lessons.analogy: Microinteractions are like the reassuring 'click' when you close a car door perfectly—it’s a tiny detail, but it subconsciously signals high build quality.
lessons.diagram:
flowchart LR
  T[Trigger] --> R[Rules]
  R --> F[Feedback]
  F --> L[Loops/Modes]

examples.what: Subtle haptics and visual spring animations when organizing tabs signal direct manipulation.
Highlights how psychological triggers map to subtle UI feedback to build habit and trust.

drills.q: What are the four components of a microinteraction?
drills.a: Trigger, Rules, Feedback, Loops/Modes.

drills.q: Why add a checkmark animation when copying code?
drills.a: To provide immediate visual confirmation, preventing the user from wondering if the action succeeded.

lessons.teachItBack: Microinteractions are the tiny moments of feedback—like a button changing to a checkmark when clicked. They consist of a trigger, rules, feedback, and loops. In AI interfaces, they provide constant, subtle reassurance that the system is responding perfectly to user actions.

glossary.definition: A contained product moment that revolves around a single use case, providing feedback or communicating status.

quiz.q: Which of the following is a classic microinteraction?
choices: A 'Copy' icon turning into a green checkmark for 2 seconds
Logging into an account via OAuth
quiz.explanation: The copy icon changing state is a single, task-based engagement that provides immediate, subtle feedback.

lessons.title: Latency Masking
lessons.summary: Using skeletons, optimistic UI, and progress states to turn waiting time into reassurance.
lessons.problem: AI operations (like agentic loops or deep reasoning) are inherently slow. Showing a blank screen or a static spinner causes users to abandon the request, thinking the system is broken.
forces: Users have low tolerance for waiting without visual feedback.
Complex AI workflows cannot be instantly sped up.
Uncertainty ('Is it stuck?') is worse than knowing a process takes time.
lessons.solution: Mask the latency by providing continuous, meaningful updates. Use optimistic UI where possible, skeleton screens, shimmering placeholders, and detailed 'thinking' states that expose intermediate steps.
tradeoffs: Exposing too many intermediate steps can confuse the user.
Optimistic UI requires complex rollback logic if the underlying operation fails.
lessons.body:
## First principles
Waiting is psychological. Ten seconds of watching a static spinner feels like a minute. Ten seconds of watching a system gather facts, read a document, and prepare an answer feels like watching an expert at work. **We mask latency by making the wait interesting and informative.**

## Skeletons and Shimmer
Before content arrives, display a skeleton screen with a subtle shimmer. This establishes the layout and signals that data is on the way, preventing jarring layout shifts when the content finally renders.

## Exposing the 'Thinking' process
For long-running tasks, show what the agent is doing. 'Searching the web...', 'Reading 3 sources...', 'Drafting response...'. This not only masks latency but builds trust by showing the AI's work.
lessons.analogy: It's like an open kitchen in a restaurant. You don't mind waiting 20 minutes for your food if you can watch the chef expertly preparing it.
lessons.diagram:
flowchart LR
  Req[Request] --> S1[Show Skeleton]
  S1 --> T1[Status: Searching...]
  T1 --> T2[Status: Reading...]
  T2 --> Res[Render Final UI]

examples.what: Shows the exact sources it is reading while constructing the answer, turning wait time into a display of competence.
Shows a dedicated, isolated loading state for code/UI artifacts while the main chat stream continues.

drills.q: What is the purpose of a skeleton screen?
drills.a: To establish layout early and signal data is loading, reducing perceived wait time and layout shift.

drills.q: How does showing intermediate steps ('thinking') mask latency?
drills.a: It gives the user something to read and builds trust, changing the perception from 'waiting' to 'watching work happen'.

lessons.teachItBack: When an AI is doing something slow, don't just show a spinner. Mask the latency by showing skeletons or narrating the steps the AI is taking. It distracts the user, proves the system is working hard, and makes the wait feel significantly shorter.

glossary.definition: Updating the UI immediately with the expected result of an action before the server confirms it.

quiz.q: Which technique is best for masking latency on a 15-second multi-step reasoning task?
choices: Optimistic UI showing the final answer
A blank screen to build suspense
quiz.explanation: Narrating the steps gives the user feedback that the system isn't stuck, making the long wait tolerable and building trust.

lessons.title: Trust and Transparency
lessons.summary: Building calibrated trust by showing the AI's work, providing citations, and communicating confidence or uncertainty.
lessons.body:
## First principles
AI systems are probabilistic, meaning they are sometimes wrong. If a system acts 100% confident 100% of the time, a single hallucination destroys all user trust. **Calibrated trust** means users trust the system when it's right, and know to verify when it's uncertain.

## Show your work
Don't just provide an answer; provide the provenance. Inline citations linked to verifiable sources allow the user to double-check the AI's claims. When the AI runs code to get an answer, show the code and the output.

## Communicating uncertainty
If the prompt is ambiguous or the data is sparse, the UX should reflect that. Use hedging language ('Based on the available documents, it seems...'), or explicitly prompt the user for clarification. Allowing users to edit the AI's memory or context further increases transparency.
lessons.analogy: A good AI is like a diligent research assistant. They don't just slide a summary under your door; they hand you the summary with footnotes pointing to the exact pages in the textbook.
lessons.diagram:
flowchart TD
  Ans[AI Answer] --> Cite[Inline Citations]
  Cite --> Src[Verifiable Sources]
  Ans --> Hed[Hedging Language if Uncertain]
  Hed --> UserVerify[User Verifies]

examples.what: Places prominent superscript citations on every factual claim, linking directly to the source.
Allows users to expand a toggle to see the exact Python code written and executed to generate a chart.

drills.q: What is 'calibrated trust'?
drills.a: When a user correctly relies on the AI when it is accurate, but knows to verify when the AI signals uncertainty.

drills.q: Why are inline citations critical for LLM UI?
drills.a: They provide provenance, allowing users to verify facts and catch hallucinations, which builds long-term trust.

lessons.teachItBack: Don't build AI UIs that pretend to be omniscient. Build transparent systems that show their work, provide citations, and admit when they are unsure. This builds 'calibrated trust'—users learn to rely on the AI safely because they can always verify its claims.

sources.title: NN/g - AI as a UX Assistant

glossary.definition: A state where a user's reliance on a system matches the system's actual reliability.

quiz.q: How should an AI UI handle a query where the underlying data is contradictory?
choices: Pick the most likely answer and state it as fact
Fail and show a generic error
Provide an answer but use hedging language and cite the contradictions
quiz.explanation: Transparency requires exposing the uncertainty and the sources, allowing the user to make the final judgment.

lessons.title: Onboarding & Progressive Disclosure
lessons.summary: Teaching capability without overwhelming the user through empty states, first-run experiences, and gradually revealing complexity.
lessons.body:
## First principles
Modern AI tools have massive, open-ended capabilities. If you show a new user every feature, setting, and plugin on day one, they will bounce due to cognitive overload. **Progressive disclosure** defers advanced features until the user actually needs them.

## The first-run experience
An empty state shouldn't be empty. It should be a guided invitation. Use starter prompts, brief product tours, or a single 'magic' button that guarantees a successful first interaction (the 'Aha!' moment).

## Gradually revealing depth
Start with a simple chat interface. Once the user masters basic queries, introduce inline editing. Later, reveal advanced settings like temperature tuning or custom system prompts. You teach the user how to drive the car before you show them how to rebuild the engine.
lessons.analogy: It's like a video game tutorial. You start in a safe room with just a 'jump' button. Only after you master jumping do the doors open to reveal enemies and complex combos.
lessons.diagram:
flowchart LR
  D1[Day 1: Starter Prompts] --> D2[Day 3: Edit & Regenerate]
  D2 --> D5[Day 10: Custom Personas]
  D5 --> D30[Day 30: API & Integrations]

examples.what: Famed for an intense, curated onboarding that ensures users master keyboard shortcuts before being left on their own.
Keeps the main UI clean, hiding advanced features like 'Projects' and 'Artifacts' until the user opts in or naturally discovers them.

drills.q: What is the danger of a literal 'empty state' in an AI app?
drills.a: It provides no affordances, leaving the user with a blank canvas problem and no idea how to start.

drills.q: Define progressive disclosure.
drills.a: An interaction design technique that sequences information and actions across several screens, reducing cognitive load by showing only what is necessary at that moment.

lessons.teachItBack: Don't overwhelm new users with settings. Use the empty state to provide guaranteed successful starter prompts. Once they get the 'aha' moment, slowly reveal advanced features over time. This progressive disclosure keeps cognitive load low while teaching the system's true power.

glossary.definition: A UI strategy of hiding advanced features until the user specifically requests or needs them.

quiz.q: What is the primary goal of the 'first-run experience'?
choices: To force the user to read the manual
To get the user to a successful 'Aha!' moment as quickly as possible
quiz.explanation: A successful first interaction proves the value of the product and motivates the user to keep learning.

lessons.title: The Ethical Hook Model
lessons.summary: Applying Nir Eyal's Hook model (Trigger → Action → Variable Reward → Investment) responsibly, ensuring delight without sliding into dark patterns.
lessons.body:
## First principles
Great products build habits. Nir Eyal's Hook Model explains how: a **Trigger** prompts an **Action**, which yields a **Variable Reward**, leading the user to make an **Investment** in the platform. In AI, this loop is incredibly powerful, but it must be wielded ethically.

## The Hook in AI
- **Trigger:** A daily digest notification, or simply the need to write an email.
- **Action:** Typing a short prompt.
- **Variable Reward:** The AI generates a surprisingly insightful, creative, or delightful response. The variability (you never know exactly how good the AI's answer will be) is psychologically engaging.
- **Investment:** The user saves the prompt, upvotes the response, or adds context to their profile, making the next loop better.

## The Regret Test
How do we separate a delightful habit from a manipulative dark pattern? Use Eyal's **Regret Test**: 'If the user knew everything the designer knows about how the product works, would they still use it, or would they regret the time spent?' Ethical AI tools empower the user to achieve their goals; manipulative tools hijack attention to maximize time-on-site.
lessons.analogy: Ethical habit-building is like a great gym routine—it's designed to keep you coming back because it genuinely improves your life. Dark patterns are like a slot machine—designed to keep you pulling the lever regardless of the outcome.
lessons.diagram:
flowchart circular
  T[Trigger: Task to do] --> A[Action: Write prompt]
  A --> V[Variable Reward: AI generates insight]
  V --> I[Investment: Save/Upvote]
  I --> T

examples.what: The foundational psychological framework for building habit-forming products.

drills.q: What are the four steps of the Hook Model?
drills.a: Trigger, Action, Variable Reward, Investment.

drills.q: What makes the reward 'variable' in LLMs?
drills.a: Because LLMs are non-deterministic, the exact phrasing, creativity, and depth of the answer changes every time, creating novelty.

lessons.teachItBack: To make a product sticky, you need a trigger that leads to a simple action, followed by a rewarding surprise, and finishing with an investment that improves the next cycle. But to do it ethically, apply the Regret Test: ensure the user actually benefits from the habit, rather than just being distracted by a dark pattern.

glossary.definition: A psychological concept where the unpredictability of a reward increases anticipation and engagement.
A UI designed to trick or manipulate users into doing things they might not want to do.

quiz.q: How do you pass the 'Regret Test'?
choices: By hiding the terms of service
If a fully informed user would not regret the time/money they spent on the product
By ensuring the user spends at least 1 hour a day in the app
By making it difficult to delete an account
quiz.explanation: Ethical design respects user agency. If they understand the system and still value the interaction, the habit is beneficial, not manipulative.

lessons.title: Error Recovery UX
lessons.summary: Designing graceful failure states, regenerate options, and honest error messages for non-deterministic AI.
lessons.problem: AI models will inevitably fail—hallucinations, context limit errors, or API timeouts. Generic 'Something went wrong' errors leave the user stuck, leading to task abandonment.
forces: AI failures are often non-deterministic and hard to predict.
Users blame themselves when they hit a dead end.
Restarting a complex prompt from scratch is highly frustrating.
lessons.solution: Design for failure. Offer one-click 'Regenerate' buttons. Preserve the user's input so they can edit and retry. Write honest, specific error messages that explain exactly what failed and offer an immediate next step.
tradeoffs: Retries cost API tokens and compute.
Exposing too much technical error detail can confuse non-technical users.
lessons.body:
## First principles
In traditional software, errors are usually deterministic bugs. In AI, errors are part of the process—the model hallucinates, the prompt is too vague, or the context window overflows. **Good UX doesn't try to eliminate all errors; it makes recovering from them effortless.**

## The anatomy of recovery
When an AI fails to generate a good response, never present a dead end. Always provide:
1. **Preservation:** Keep the user's original text intact.
2. **Explanation:** Be honest ('The document was too long for me to read completely').
3. **Action:** Provide a clear way out ('Try summarizing a smaller section', or 'Regenerate response').

## Undo and Regenerate
The simplest form of error recovery is the 'Regenerate' button. Because LLMs are probabilistic, the exact same prompt might yield a much better answer on the second try. Pair this with a mechanism to view previous generations so users aren't afraid of losing a 'pretty good' answer to try for a 'perfect' one.
lessons.analogy: When a GPS loses signal, it doesn't just turn off the screen. It says 'Recalculating...' and tries to find a new route based on your last known location.
lessons.diagram:
flowchart TD
  Err[Error/Bad Generation] --> Keep[Preserve User Input]
  Keep --> Msg[Explain Issue Honestly]
  Msg --> Act[Provide Action: Edit / Regenerate]
  Act --> Retry[Retry Request]

examples.what: Provides a 'Regenerate' button and allows pagination through previous generations so you never lose work.

drills.q: Why is 'Regenerate' a valid error recovery strategy in AI?
drills.a: Because LLMs are non-deterministic, a second attempt at the exact same prompt might yield a better or correct result.

drills.q: What is the worst thing a UI can do during an error?
drills.a: Delete the user's input and show a generic 'Something went wrong' message, creating a dead end.

lessons.teachItBack: AI makes mistakes. Instead of hiding that fact, build UI that makes bouncing back easy. Keep their text, tell them exactly why it failed, and give them a one-click way to try again. Make failure a bump in the road, not a dead end.

glossary.definition: A system's ability to maintain limited functionality even when a large portion of it has been destroyed or rendered inoperative.

quiz.q: If a user uploads a PDF that exceeds the context window, what is the best UX response?
choices: The file is too large. Please upload a smaller file.
Ignore the error and summarize the first 10%
Your PDF is 50 pages, but I can only read 20 at a time. Do you want me to summarize the first 20 pages, or would you like to upload a smaller section?
quiz.explanation: Option D preserves context, explains the exact limitation honestly, and provides immediate, actionable next steps without creating a dead end.

lessons.title: Command Palettes & Keyboard-First UX
lessons.summary: Reducing time-to-action for power users through Cmd-K interfaces and keyboard shortcuts.
lessons.problem: Clicking through nested menus to trigger AI actions breaks flow state. Power users abandon tools that force them to use the mouse for repetitive tasks.
forces: Users want to stay in flow state while coding or writing.
GUI menus are discoverable but slow.
Keyboard shortcuts are fast but hard to discover and memorize.
lessons.solution: Implement a universally accessible Command Palette (Cmd/Ctrl + K) that acts as a fuzzy-searchable index of all AI actions. Pair this with extensive keyboard shortcuts for power users.
tradeoffs: Command palettes require good fuzzy search to be effective.
They are hidden by default, requiring onboarding for discoverability.
lessons.body:
## First principles
Speed is a feature. When a tool matches the speed of thought, users achieve flow state. Moving the hand from the keyboard to the mouse introduces friction. **Keyboard-first design minimizes the gap between intention and action.**

## The Cmd-K paradigm
The Command Palette has become the universal 'do anything' interface. It solves the discoverability vs. speed tradeoff. A user hits Cmd-K, types 'sum...', and hits Enter to summarize a document. The UI shows the specific keyboard shortcut next to the action, teaching the user to be even faster next time.

## Design for flow
AI tools should sit in the background until summoned. A global shortcut to summon an AI chat (like Raycast), or inline generation triggered by a hotkey (like GitHub Copilot), ensures the AI is always exactly one keystroke away.
lessons.analogy: A command palette is like the spotlight search on your computer: instead of digging through folders to find an app, you just type its name and it appears instantly.
lessons.diagram:
flowchart LR
  Int[Intention: Summarize] --> Cmd[Press Cmd-K]
  Cmd --> Type[Type 'sum']
  Type --> Act[Select Action + Learn Shortcut]
  Act --> Flow[Return to Flow]

examples.what: The entire product is built around a lightning-fast, keyboard-driven command palette.
Famed for its Cmd-K menu that allows users to perform any action without touching the mouse.

drills.q: How does a Command Palette solve the 'discoverability vs speed' tradeoff?
drills.a: It allows fast keyboard execution (speed) while providing a searchable list of all commands (discoverability).

drills.q: How do you teach power-users faster shortcuts?
drills.a: By displaying the direct keyboard shortcut right-aligned next to the action inside the Command Palette.

lessons.teachItBack: Don't make power users click through menus. Give them a Cmd-K command palette where they can fuzzy search every AI capability instantly. It keeps their hands on the keyboard, preserves their flow state, and organically teaches them faster shortcuts.

sources.title: Command palettes are the new standard

glossary.definition: A searchable, keyboard-driven menu that provides access to an application's commands and features.

quiz.q: Why is minimizing mouse usage important for power users?
choices: Moving from keyboard to mouse and back breaks cognitive flow and physical rhythm
Keyboard events are easier to track in analytics
quiz.explanation: The physical act of switching input devices introduces latency and breaks the 'speed of thought' flow state.

lessons.title: Feedback and Steering
lessons.summary: Providing tools for users to guide the AI, correct its course, and implicitly train the system through thumbs, edits, and semantic steering.
lessons.body:
## First principles
An AI's first guess is rarely perfect. If the only option is to accept it or abandon it, the UX fails. **Steering** provides low-friction ways for the user to tweak the output (e.g., 'make it shorter', 'more professional'). **Feedback** (thumbs up/down) lets the system learn for next time.

## Low-friction steering
Instead of making the user write a new prompt ('Rewrite that but make it sound less formal and cut it in half'), provide semantic buttons: [Shorter], [More Professional], [Bullet points]. This turns prompt engineering into simple clicks.

## Closing the loop
When a user edits an AI's output, that is the ultimate signal of what they actually wanted. A great system captures those diffs implicitly as training data (or context for the next turn), rather than relying solely on explicit thumbs up/down, which have low completion rates.
lessons.analogy: Steering an AI should feel like giving notes to an eager intern. You don't rewrite the whole report yourself; you hand it back and say 'great start, just make the intro punchier,' and they do the rest.
lessons.diagram:
flowchart TD
  Gen[Initial Generation] --> Eval{User Evaluation}
  Eval -- Needs Tweak --> Steer[Click: Make Shorter]
  Steer --> Gen2[New Generation]
  Eval -- Perfect --> Implicit[User Copies/Saves (Implicit +1)]
  Eval -- Terrible --> Explicit[User Thumbs Down]

examples.what: Offers a dropdown of steering options (Make longer, Fix spelling, Change tone) directly over highlighted text.
Provides explicit feedback mechanisms (thumbs up/down) that directly inform their RLHF alignment models.

drills.q: What is the difference between implicit and explicit feedback?
drills.a: Explicit is a direct action like clicking a thumbs-up. Implicit is inferred from user behavior, like copying the text or making an edit.

drills.q: Why are semantic steering buttons better than asking the user to re-prompt?
drills.a: They drastically lower the cognitive load and physical friction required to iterate on an answer.

lessons.teachItBack: AI outputs are drafts. You need to give users the steering wheel to adjust them easily—buttons for 'shorter' or 'friendlier' beat forcing them to type new prompts. And you must capture their edits and feedback to make the system smarter for their next session.

sources.title: NN/g - AI as a UX Assistant

glossary.definition: The UX mechanisms that allow a user to adjust and refine AI output without writing complete new prompts.
Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback; the process of using human ratings to train an AI on what good answers look like.

quiz.q: Which of the following is an example of an 'implicit' positive feedback signal?
choices: Copying the generated code block to the clipboard
quiz.explanation: Copying the output strongly implies the user found it valuable enough to use, without them having to explicitly rate it.

lessons.title: WHOOP — Biometric Context as Coaching
lessons.summary: WHOOP turns continuous biometric signals — recovery, strain, sleep, HRV, stress, and journal behaviors — into a conversational AI coach that can explain what is happening and suggest what to do next.
contextSignals: Sleep duration, sleep need, and sleep quality
Heart-rate variability (HRV) and resting heart rate
Goals and planned events entered by the member
WHOOP Journal behaviors such as diet, medication, alcohol, travel, hydration, and other logged routines
Comparisons to similar members when available
lessons.analogy: A coach who has watched your sleep, training, stress, and habits for months — not a generic chatbot reading a fitness article.
lessons.diagram:
flowchart LR
  A[Wearable sensors: HRV, sleep, strain, stress] --> B[WHOOP algorithms and member history]
  C[Goals and Journal behaviors] --> B
  B --> D{What changed or what is the user asking?}
  D --> E[Retrieve relevant biometric trends and performance science]
  E --> F[GPT-4 powered WHOOP Coach]
  F --> G[Explain why: recovery, readiness, sleep debt]
  F --> H[Recommend action: train, rest, hydrate, adjust sleep]
  G --> I[Member behavior next day]
  H --> I
  I --> A
lessons.body:
## What it is
WHOOP is a wearable performance platform. The teardown-worthy move is WHOOP Coach: a GPT-4-powered conversational coach that sits on top of a member's biometric history, goals, WHOOP algorithms, and performance science. WHOOP says Coach identifies patterns and connections in Recovery, Strain, Sleep, Health, and Stress data, then produces individualized responses in seconds. Press coverage from Wareable described the feature as a ChatGPT-style coach that can summarize recent sleep, strain, and recovery performance, answer follow-ups, and link users back into relevant WHOOP app screens.

That matters because the product does not ask the model to be a generic fitness expert. It gives the model a dense context layer: your actual sleep, HRV, strain, stress, and logged behaviors. The AI experience feels useful because it can answer questions like "why am I so tired?" with personal evidence rather than generic advice.

## Context signals it uses
The core signals are biometric and behavioral: Recovery, Strain, Sleep, Health, Stress, HRV, goals, and WHOOP Journal entries. WHOOP says members can track 140+ custom behaviors, from diet to medication, and use those to uncover effects on physiological metrics. That turns the Journal into a causal-hypothesis surface: "When I drink alcohol, travel, or change bedtime, what happens to recovery?"

The richest signal is not any one metric; it is the relationship between them. Sleep debt changes recovery. Strain changes sleep need. HRV and resting heart rate change the interpretation of readiness. A coach can be proactive because it sees the before-and-after pattern across days, not only a static dashboard.

## Patterns under the hood
WHOOP Coach is a strong example of RAG over personal data. The retrieval layer is the member's biometrics, journal entries, goals, and performance-science explanations. Tool-use shows up as domain calculations: sleep need, strain, recovery, comparison cohorts, and membership support. Long-term memory is implicit in the historical wearable record.

The product also needs routing and guardrails. Some questions are coaching questions, some are education questions, and some are support questions. Health-adjacent advice should be bounded: explain patterns, recommend behavior changes, and avoid pretending to diagnose disease.

## UX & engagement moves
WHOOP already has a daily ritual: wake up, check recovery, decide how hard to push. Coach makes that ritual conversational. Instead of forcing users to interpret charts, it lets them ask natural questions and receive a clear, personalized explanation.

The trust move is specificity. A useful answer should name the signal it used: "your HRV is below baseline," "sleep was short," or "strain has been high for three days." Latency masking also matters: WHOOP emphasizes answers within seconds, so the AI feels like an always-available coach rather than a report generator.

## What to steal (lessons)
Do not bolt a chatbot onto a dashboard. Build a context contract first: what signals are reliable, what they mean, and how they combine. Then let the AI translate those signals into action.

Make the recommendation explainable. The best engagement loop is not "AI says rest"; it is "AI says rest because sleep debt rose, HRV dropped, and yesterday's strain was high." Finally, create a journal or feedback loop so the user can add missing context and the system improves over time.

examples.what: Uses member goals, biometric data, WHOOP algorithms, performance science, and GPT-4 to answer health and fitness questions with individualized guidance.
Frames WHOOP as an LLM-powered health solution built around personalized member data rather than generic answers.
Reported that WHOOP Coach can summarize recent sleep, strain, and recovery performance, answer follow-up questions, and direct users to relevant app screens.

drills.q: What makes WHOOP Coach feel more contextual than a normal fitness chatbot?
drills.a: It can ground answers in the member's actual recovery, sleep, HRV, strain, stress, goals, and logged behaviors.

drills.q: What should a health-adjacent AI product explain before recommending action?
drills.a: The signals it used, the pattern it detected, the confidence or limits of the interpretation, and the concrete next step.

lessons.teachItBack: WHOOP Coach works because the AI is wrapped around a rich biometric context layer. It does not just answer fitness questions; it interprets sleep, recovery, HRV, strain, stress, and habits together. The lesson is to make context visible, then translate it into a specific behavior change.

sources.title: WHOOP Unveils the New WHOOP Coach Powered by OpenAI
Wareable — Whoop launches GPT-4 AI coach

glossary.definition: A WHOOP readiness signal that summarizes how prepared the body appears to be for strain, informed by physiological metrics such as HRV and resting heart rate.
Continuous physiological data used as input for personalization, explanations, and recommendations.

quiz.q: What is the key product lesson from WHOOP Coach?
choices: Use AI as a generic content generator
Ground coaching in live and historical biometric signals
quiz.explanation: WHOOP Coach is powerful because it uses real member data — sleep, recovery, HRV, strain, stress, goals, and behaviors — as context for coaching.

lessons.title: Google Gemini — Multimodal Context with Motion as Feedback
lessons.summary: Gemini pairs multimodal inputs — text, audio, image, video, screen, and tools — with a visual language of gradients, containers, and motion that makes AI feel responsive instead of static.
contextSignals: Video streams and real-time camera context
Screen or app context in developer demos
Latency and turn-taking signals in live multimodal sessions
User intent inferred from modality choice
lessons.analogy: A translator in a glass studio: it listens, watches, searches, and uses motion to show that the system is alive and tracking your context.
lessons.diagram:
flowchart TD
  T[Text prompt] --> C[Gemini context window]
  I[Image or screenshot] --> C
  A[Audio or voice] --> C
  V[Video stream] --> C
  C --> R{Route by modality and intent}
  R --> S[Search grounding or tools]
  R --> M[Multimodal model reasoning]
  S --> M
  M --> O[Answer: text, voice, visual structure]
  O --> UX[Motion, gradient, streaming feedback]
  UX --> U[User continues or interrupts]
lessons.body:
## What it is
Gemini is Google's family of multimodal AI models and product experiences. The important teardown angle is not only that Gemini can work with text, images, audio, and video. It is that Google treats the response experience as a designed interface: visual identity, responsive containers, gradients, streaming, and motion all communicate state.

Google's Gemini announcement positioned the model as natively multimodal. The Gemini 2.0 Multimodal Live API adds bidirectional streaming of text, audio, and video, sub-second first-token latency, voice activity detection, interruption, video understanding, tool integration, search grounding, and code execution.

## Context signals it uses
Gemini can take many context signals at once: text instructions, visual input, audio, video, and tool results. In a live interaction, the system can also infer intent from turn-taking: whether the user interrupted, paused, pointed a camera, or continued speaking.

That changes the product shape. A typed assistant waits for a prompt. A multimodal assistant can interpret the scene, the user's voice, and the task simultaneously. The context surface becomes less like a chat box and more like a sensorium.

## Patterns under the hood
Routing is the key architecture pattern. A Gemini experience must decide whether to answer from the model, ground via Search, call a tool, execute code, or combine modalities in the same request. The Live API documentation explicitly describes tool integration, function calling, code execution, search grounding, and combinations of tools inside one request.

RAG matters when Search grounding is used. Guardrails matter because multimodal inputs raise new failure modes: mistaken visual interpretation, overconfident search synthesis, and privacy-sensitive screen or camera context.

## UX & engagement moves
Motion is the differentiator to study. Google Design's Gemini visual-design writeup says gradients, responsive containers, and intentional motion work together to create magic, clarity, familiarity, and trust. Material motion principles give those state changes a grammar: continuity, feedback, and purposeful transitions.

Streaming and low latency make the assistant feel present. Motion should not be decoration; it should explain state. Listening, processing, grounding, and responding can each have a different visual cadence. This helps users understand that multimodal AI is doing work without forcing them to read technical logs.

## What to steal (lessons)
If your agent sees more than text, design more than text. Use motion to reveal state: listening, looking, searching, composing, and done. Give the user interruption and correction paths so multimodality does not feel like surveillance or inevitability.

Also steal the routing mindset. Multimodal context is expensive and noisy. The product should choose the smallest useful context and the right tool path, then make that choice legible through the interface.

examples.what: Supports bidirectional streaming with text, audio, video, low latency, tool integration, search grounding, and code execution.
Uses gradients, responsive containers, and intentional motion to make the evolving AI assistant feel intuitive and trustworthy.

drills.q: Why does motion matter in Gemini-like AI interfaces?
drills.a: It communicates state — listening, processing, grounding, responding — so users can follow what the assistant is doing.

drills.q: What is the architectural challenge of multimodal context?
drills.a: Routing: deciding which modalities and tools are relevant without overwhelming the model or the user.

lessons.teachItBack: Gemini is a multimodal assistant, so its context can include text, image, audio, video, screen, and tool results. Google pairs that capability with motion and responsive visuals so the product feels alive and understandable. The key lesson is that multimodal AI needs multimodal feedback.

sources.title: Google — Making Gemini available to the world
Google Developers Blog — Gemini 2.0 real-time multimodal interactions
Google Design — Gemini AI visual design

glossary.definition: Inputs from multiple modalities such as text, audio, image, video, screen, and tool results used together in one AI interaction.
Animation that communicates interface state, continuity, and progress rather than merely decorating the UI.

quiz.q: What should motion communicate in a multimodal AI response?
quiz.explanation: In Gemini-like interfaces, motion is useful when it shows state such as listening, processing, grounding, and responding.

lessons.title: Claude — Workspaces, Artifacts, and Transparent Control
lessons.summary: Claude turns a chat into a collaborative workspace through Artifacts, Projects, long context, tool use, and carefully framed computer-use capabilities.
contextSignals: Project knowledge: uploaded docs, code, insights, and custom instructions
Screenshots and UI state for computer use
Mouse, cursor, click, and typing feedback in computer-use loops
lessons.analogy: A careful teammate who keeps the shared project binder open, drafts in a side window, and asks for the controls before touching the computer.
lessons.diagram:
flowchart LR
  U[User goal] --> P[Project knowledge and instructions]
  P --> L[Long context assembly]
  U --> C[Conversation]
  C --> L
  L --> D{Output type?}
  D --> A[Artifact: code, doc, diagram, app]
  D --> T[Tool or computer-use action]
  D --> R[Plain explanation]
  T --> O[Observe screen or tool result]
  O --> L
  A --> E[User reviews and edits]
  E --> L
lessons.body:
## What it is
Claude has evolved from a chat surface into a workspace. Artifacts put generated code, documents, diagrams, websites, and dashboards in a dedicated side window. Projects collect chats, knowledge, documents, code, and custom instructions so Claude can start with the right background instead of a cold prompt.

Anthropic also introduced computer use in public beta, letting developers direct Claude to use computers by observing a screen, moving a cursor, clicking, and typing. Combined with long context, the product direction is clear: Claude is designed to work with large bodies of material, produce durable work objects, and act through controlled tools.

## Context signals it uses
Projects are the clearest context signal. Anthropic describes Projects as curated sets of knowledge and chat activity with a 200K context window in the launch post, suitable for style guides, codebases, interview transcripts, and past work. Claude can also use custom instructions to adopt a role, tone, or domain stance inside a project.

Artifacts add a second context loop. The user can see and edit the work product directly, and that object becomes the shared reference. Computer use adds UI-state context: screenshots, cursor position, clicked elements, typed text, and subsequent observations.

## Patterns under the hood
Claude uses long-context retrieval and compression patterns to keep large work sessions usable. Projects are long-term memory at the workspace level. Artifacts are a tool-output pattern: the model does not just answer; it creates an object that can be inspected and iterated.

Computer use is ReAct-like tool use over a graphical interface. The loop is observe screen, decide next action, click or type, observe again. Anthropic's launch post is explicit that computer use was experimental and could be cumbersome or error-prone, which is a transparency and guardrails pattern rather than a marketing gloss.

## UX & engagement moves
The strongest UX move is side-by-side collaboration. Artifacts reduce the chat scroll problem by giving important outputs a stable surface. This increases trust because users can review the artifact as an object, not hunt through a transcript.

Claude also leans into transparency. Projects tell users what context they are giving Claude. Computer use is framed as an early, experimental capability. Privacy notes in Anthropic's posts emphasize that user data is not used for training without explicit consent in stated contexts.

## What to steal (lessons)
For serious work, chat alone is not enough. Give the agent a durable workspace, durable context, and durable outputs. Let users inspect and edit the result while the conversation continues.

Also copy the caution. If an agent can operate a computer, say exactly what it can do, how it observes, and where it is unreliable. Trust comes from bounded power, not theatrical autonomy.

examples.what: Generated work appears in a dedicated window alongside conversation, enabling users to see, edit, and build on the output.
Claude can be directed through an API to look at a screen, move a cursor, click buttons, and type text in an experimental public beta.

drills.q: Why are Artifacts more than a visual flourish?
drills.a: They make generated work durable, inspectable, editable, and separate from the chat transcript.

drills.q: What is the core agent loop in Claude computer use?
drills.a: Observe the screen, decide the next UI action, click or type, observe the result, and repeat with guardrails.

lessons.teachItBack: Claude teaches that agentic products need workspaces, not just chat bubbles. Projects provide persistent context, while Artifacts turn outputs into editable objects. Computer use extends the agent loop into UI actions, but Anthropic frames it with clear caveats and controls.

sources.title: Anthropic — Claude 3.5 models and computer use

glossary.definition: A Claude output shown as a persistent, editable object in a dedicated workspace beside the conversation.
A tool-use capability where an AI observes a computer interface and issues mouse and keyboard actions.

quiz.q: What product problem do Claude Artifacts solve?
choices: They turn important outputs into stable editable work objects
They remove the need for context
quiz.explanation: Artifacts create a durable side-by-side surface for code, documents, diagrams, apps, and other outputs.

lessons.title: Cursor — Repo Context as the Interface
lessons.summary: Cursor makes the codebase itself the context layer: indexes, embeddings, editor state, linter errors, Tab completions, and agents all work together to predict the next useful code action.
contextSignals: Merkle-tree file hashes for index sync
Semantic search results across the repository
User accept/reject feedback on Tab suggestions
Agent task prompt, working set, terminal output, and test results
lessons.analogy: An IDE that reads the room: it watches your cursor, knows the repo map, notices the linter, and suggests the next edit before you ask.
lessons.diagram:
flowchart TD
  F[Files in workspace] --> I[Index: chunks, hashes, embeddings]
  E[Editor state: cursor, recent edits, diagnostics] --> P[Prompt/context builder]
  I --> P
  P --> R{Need fast edit or task?}
  R --> T[Cursor Tab suggestion]
  R --> A[Agent mode: search, edit, run commands]
  T --> U[Developer accepts, rejects, or keeps typing]
  A --> O[Terminal/test/tool observations]
  O --> A
  U --> L[Feedback signals improve future behavior]
lessons.body:
## What it is
Cursor is an AI code editor whose core product insight is that code assistance is only as good as repo context. Cursor Tab is AI-powered autocomplete that suggests code as you type using recent edits, surrounding code, and linter errors. Cursor's indexing work turns the broader codebase into semantic search context for agents.

The product is not one feature. It is a stack: instant inline completions, cross-file edit prediction, semantic codebase search, and agents that can search, edit, run commands, and return work for review.

## Context signals it uses
The fast path is editor context: cursor position, nearby code, recent edits, and diagnostics. The Tab docs say suggestions appear as grayed-out text and can edit multiple lines, add missing imports, predict next locations, and even suggest cross-file edits.

The deep path is indexing. Cursor's secure codebase indexing post explains that it builds a searchable index when a project opens, splits changed files into syntactic chunks, converts chunks into embeddings, and uses Merkle trees and hashes to sync changes efficiently. That gives the AI a map of where concepts live, not just what is visible on screen.

## Patterns under the hood
Cursor is a productized RAG system over code. Semantic search retrieves relevant chunks; the model uses those chunks plus editor state to answer or edit. Tool use appears when agents read files, edit files, search the repo, and run terminal commands.

Reflection shows up in agent loops: run a command, see an error, change code, run again. Routing decides whether a task belongs to Tab, chat, agent mode, semantic search, or a terminal action.

## UX & engagement moves
Cursor Tab is ghost text with a high bar for interruption. Cursor's reinforcement-learning post says low-accept suggestions are distracting, so the system learns when to show nothing. That is a major UX lesson: silence can be an AI feature.

Latency masking is also central. Tab is useful because it is nearly instant. Cursor's Fusion post reports lower server latency and longer context than earlier Tab models. For agents, the UX shifts from keystroke assistance to supervised delegation: users review diffs, logs, and artifacts instead of typing every line.

## What to steal (lessons)
Context beats clever prompts. If you want an AI developer tool to feel magical, invest in indexing, diagnostics, recent-edit state, and fast retrieval. Then decide which surface should answer: inline ghost text for low-friction edits, chat for explanation, agent mode for multi-step work.

Also steal the acceptance discipline. If your assistant is not confident, it should not interrupt. Great AI UX is often about knowing when to stay quiet.

examples.what: Builds searchable codebase indexes with syntactic chunks, embeddings, Merkle trees, and access proofs for fast semantic search.
Uses recent edits, surrounding code, and linter errors to suggest grayed-out completions, multi-line edits, imports, jumps, and cross-file edits.

drills.q: Why is codebase indexing more important than a bigger prompt alone?
drills.a: Indexing retrieves the relevant code chunks from a large repo instead of stuffing unrelated files into context.

drills.q: What UX principle does Cursor Tab demonstrate?
drills.a: Only interrupt when useful; a bad ghost-text suggestion is worse than silence.

lessons.teachItBack: Cursor works because it treats the repository as live context, not static text. Tab handles fast in-flow edits, while semantic indexing and agents handle larger tasks. The product lesson is to match the context depth to the interaction surface.

sources.title: Cursor — Improving Cursor Tab with online RL
Cursor — The third era of AI software development

glossary.definition: Retrieval that finds code by meaning using embeddings, not only by exact text match.
A low-friction inline suggestion shown in the editor before the user accepts it.

quiz.q: Which context signal is especially important for Cursor Tab?
choices: Recent edits, surrounding code, and linter errors
quiz.explanation: Cursor Tab is designed around local editor state plus diagnostics and repo context.

lessons.title: Perplexity — Search, Cite, and Refine
lessons.summary: Perplexity productizes the retrieve → reason → cite loop: it searches the live web, composes direct answers, exposes sources, and lets users refine the source domain with Focus.
contextSignals: Follow-up questions in the current thread
Source type selected by Focus, such as academic or social
Pro Search decomposition for deeper searches
Premium data-provider availability for some domains
lessons.analogy: A research assistant who answers first, but staples the source packet to every claim.
lessons.diagram:
flowchart LR
  Q[User question] --> F{Focus selected?}
  F -->|Web/Academic/Social/etc.| S[Retrieve live sources]
  S --> C[Rank and choose evidence]
  C --> A[Compose concise answer]
  A --> X[Attach clickable citations]
  X --> U[User reads or opens sources]
  U --> R[Follow-up question]
  R --> S
lessons.body:
## What it is
Perplexity calls itself an answer engine. Its core promise is simple: ask a question, get a direct answer, and see the sources. The official getting-started guide says Perplexity searches the internet in real time and includes sources and citations with answers.

That makes the product feel different from both classic search and ordinary chat. Search gives links without synthesis. Chat gives synthesis without enough provenance. Perplexity tries to combine the two into a research loop.

## Context signals it uses
The initial context is the query. Then Perplexity adds retrieved web sources, follow-up questions, file uploads, and Focus mode. The guide describes Focus as a way to narrow results to specific sources such as academic journals or social media. It also describes Pro Search as a deeper mode that breaks questions into smaller steps and pulls from a broader range of sources.

Citations are context signals too. They tell the user where each answer came from and give the system a visible accountability layer. Premium source partnerships add another signal: for health, finance, statistics, market data, and educational content, the product can route to more authoritative datasets when available.

## Patterns under the hood
Perplexity is a textbook RAG product. Retrieve sources, synthesize the answer, cite evidence, then let the user refine with follow-ups. The loop is ReAct-like because the answer depends on live search actions and observations rather than model memory alone.

Routing is important. A query about academic literature should not use the same source pool as a query about social sentiment. Guardrails show up through citation visibility and domain-specific cautions, especially in health contexts where Perplexity says answers include citations and care-seeking guidance.

## UX & engagement moves
The signature UX move is source-first trust. Clickable citations make verification part of the normal reading flow. Users do not have to ask "where did that come from?" because the product puts the source next to the claim.

Focus is a second strong move. It gives users an understandable control for retrieval scope without exposing the retrieval stack. Instead of tuning embeddings or ranking weights, users choose the kind of source they want.

## What to steal (lessons)
If your AI product uses retrieval, expose provenance. Citations are not decoration; they are the trust interface for RAG. Let users refine the corpus in human terms: academic, news, social, docs, file, workspace.

Also keep the answer compact. Perplexity works because it reduces the link-hunting cost while keeping the escape hatch open: every claim can lead back to source material.

examples.what: Provides real-time answers with clickable citations and supports follow-up questions in a conversational thread.
Integrates premium content providers such as Statista, PitchBook, and Wiley to cite trusted data in selected domains.

drills.q: Why do citations change the UX of AI search?
drills.a: They turn trust into an inspectable interface: users can verify the source behind a claim.

drills.q: What does Focus mode teach product designers?
drills.a: Expose retrieval control in user-friendly source categories rather than technical knobs.

lessons.teachItBack: Perplexity is a RAG product wrapped in a search UX. It retrieves live sources, writes a concise answer, and makes citations visible. The lesson is that grounded AI should let users inspect and steer the evidence base.

sources.title: Perplexity — More Value In Every Answer

glossary.definition: A search experience that synthesizes a direct answer while preserving links to underlying sources.
A user-facing control that narrows retrieval to a source type or domain.

quiz.q: What is Perplexity's central trust move?
quiz.explanation: Perplexity makes source verification a first-class part of the answer interface.

lessons.title: ChatGPT — Memory, Canvas, Voice, and Tools
lessons.summary: ChatGPT layers persistent memory, project-like Canvas workspaces, voice conversations, and tools into one assistant that can personalize, collaborate, and act.
contextSignals: Connected Gmail context when enabled and available
Highlighted Canvas sections and full project content
Voice audio, transcripts, and in some modes video or screen context
Tool outputs such as browsing, code execution, or app connectors
lessons.analogy: A personal assistant with a notepad, a drafting table, a phone call, and a toolbox — all in the same room.
lessons.diagram:
flowchart TD
  U[User request] --> M{Need personalization?}
  M -->|yes| MEM[Saved memories, past chats, files, connected apps]
  M -->|no| C[Current chat]
  MEM --> R[Context selection]
  C --> R
  R --> D{Best surface?}
  D --> A[Chat answer]
  D --> V[Voice conversation]
  D --> CAN[Canvas document/code workspace]
  D --> T[Tool or agent action]
  T --> O[Observation/result]
  O --> R
  CAN --> E[User direct edits and highlights]
  E --> R
lessons.body:
## What it is
ChatGPT is no longer just a chat transcript. It is a container for multiple interaction modes: memory for personalization, Canvas for writing and coding projects, voice for spoken dialogue, and tools for actions beyond text.

The product lesson is surface routing. Some tasks should stay in chat. Some need a side-by-side editable workspace. Some are faster by voice. Some require tool calls or connected apps. The assistant feels broad because it can choose the interaction shape as well as the answer.

## Context signals it uses
OpenAI's memory help says saved memories are details ChatGPT can use in future responses, and that users can view, delete, or turn them off. The same help page describes memory sources, including saved memories, past chats, custom instructions, and for some users files or Gmail if connected.

Canvas adds project context. The Canvas help page says users can highlight specific sections so ChatGPT knows what to focus on, and that ChatGPT can give inline feedback with the entire project in mind. Voice adds turn-taking, audio, transcription, and retention considerations.

## Patterns under the hood
Memory-long-term is the obvious pattern. It personalizes future answers, but it must be paired with transparency and user controls. Tool-use and routing decide whether to answer directly, open Canvas, use a connected app, browse, run code, or continue a voice session.

Reflection shows up in Canvas workflows: review code, suggest edits, fix bugs, adjust length, restore versions, and iterate. Guardrails matter because the assistant can reference personal context and interact with web previews or connected services.

## UX & engagement moves
Memory reduces repetition. Canvas reduces chat-scroll friction by making work editable and versioned. Voice reduces input friction and makes the assistant feel present. Each move increases engagement by matching the modality to the job.

The trust move is control. Users can manage memories, see memory sources in some experiences, highlight exactly what Canvas should modify, and confirm risky web-preview communications. Personalization is only delightful if users understand and can change what the system remembers.

## What to steal (lessons)
Build multiple surfaces for different task shapes. Do not force code review, long-form drafting, voice dialogue, and personal reminders into the same chat box. Route to the right surface.

Also make personalization inspectable. If the system remembers, show what it remembers, where it came from, and how to remove it. Memory without controls feels creepy; memory with controls feels helpful.

examples.what: Uses saved memories and relevant past context to personalize responses, with controls to view, delete, or disable memory.
Provides a side-by-side workspace where users can edit directly, highlight sections, request inline feedback, review code, and restore versions.

drills.q: What makes memory a UX risk as well as a feature?
drills.a: Personal context can feel invasive unless users can see, edit, delete, and disable it.

drills.q: Why does Canvas belong outside the normal chat stream?
drills.a: Writing and coding projects need stable editable state, targeted selections, and versioning, not only messages.

lessons.teachItBack: ChatGPT is a multi-surface assistant. Memory personalizes, Canvas supports durable editing, voice lowers input friction, and tools let the system act. The design lesson is to route each task to the surface that makes control and context clearest.

sources.title: OpenAI — Memory and new controls for ChatGPT
OpenAI — New tools for building agents

glossary.definition: A user-specific detail ChatGPT may use in future conversations when memory is enabled.
A ChatGPT workspace for drafting, editing, reviewing, and iterating on writing or code outside the linear chat transcript.

quiz.q: What is the safest way to make AI memory feel useful?
choices: Provide user controls and source visibility
quiz.explanation: Memory becomes trustworthy when users can inspect and manage what is remembered and how it is used.

lessons.title: GitHub Copilot — IDE Context and Ghost Text
lessons.summary: GitHub Copilot uses the developer’s IDE context — current file, cursor, suffix, open tabs, selected files, chat context, and workspace signals — to generate inline suggestions, chat answers, edits, and agentic workflows.
contextSignals: Current file prefix and suffix around the cursor
Cursor location and active editor state
Relevant repositories, files, folders, symbols, and codebase context
Chat history and explicitly added context
Terminal output and tool results in agent workflows
Issue, PR, and custom-instruction context for cloud/agent tasks
lessons.analogy: A pair programmer looking over your shoulder, aware of the file, the neighboring tabs, the chat, and the tests you are about to run.
lessons.diagram:
flowchart LR
  IDE[IDE state: cursor, prefix, suffix] --> P[Prompt/context assembly]
  Tabs[Open files / neighboring tabs] --> P
  Chat[Chat prompt and selected context] --> P
  P --> D{Interaction surface}
  D --> G[Ghost-text completion]
  D --> C[Copilot Chat answer]
  D --> E[Multi-file edits]
  D --> A[Agent mode]
  A --> T[Run/read/edit/tool observations]
  T --> A
  G --> U[Developer accepts, rejects, or edits]
  E --> U
  C --> U
lessons.body:
## What it is
GitHub Copilot is an AI coding assistant across IDEs, GitHub, and agentic workflows. The classic magic is ghost text: Copilot offers grayed-out inline code suggestions as you type, accepted with Tab. The newer surface is Copilot Chat, Edits, and agent mode.

The important teardown is context assembly. Copilot does not simply send the last line you typed. GitHub's engineering blog explains prompt construction from IDE code and relevant context, including neighboring tabs and fill-in-the-middle.

## Context signals it uses
Copilot uses immediate editor context: code before and after the cursor, comments, active file, and language. GitHub's docs say inline suggestions are best for snippets, variable names, functions, repetitive code, tests, and natural-language comments.

The GitHub blog describes neighboring tabs as a technique that lets Copilot process all open files in the IDE, not just the active file. Best-practice docs tell users to open relevant files and close irrelevant ones. Chat and VS Code context tools add explicit references to files, folders, symbols, terminal output, source control changes, and codebase context.

## Patterns under the hood
Copilot uses RAG-like context selection over editor and repository signals. Chat and agent mode add tool use: read files, edit code, run commands, and self-correct after errors. Agent mode is planner-executor plus reflection: break down the task, make changes, observe errors, remediate, and continue.

Guardrails are product and workflow guardrails. The docs repeatedly remind users to review and validate Copilot's suggestions, run tests, and keep the human in charge.

## UX & engagement moves
Ghost text is a low-friction UX masterpiece. It appears exactly where work is happening, can be accepted with Tab, partially accepted, ignored, or replaced. It does not require a separate chat ritual.

Chat and Edits handle tasks that need more explicit context and review. Agent mode changes the engagement loop again: the user states a task, Copilot streams edits, may suggest or run commands depending on environment, and the user reviews results.

## What to steal (lessons)
Match autonomy to context and reversibility. Inline suggestions should be fast and easy to ignore. Multi-file edits should be reviewable. Agents should expose steps, terminal output, and final changes.

Also teach users how to shape context. Copilot's own best-practice docs say to open relevant files and close irrelevant ones. Great AI products do not hide context management; they make it a learnable skill.

examples.what: Shows grayed-out code suggestions in the IDE as the user types, accepted with Tab and adjustable through alternatives or partial acceptance.
GitHub describes using open IDE tabs as context so Copilot can include relevant matches from files beyond the active editor.

drills.q: Why do neighboring tabs improve Copilot suggestions?
drills.a: They give the model relevant code from open files, not only the active file, so suggestions better match project patterns.

drills.q: What is the UX advantage of ghost text?
drills.a: It is immediate, in-place, reversible, and easy to ignore or accept without leaving the editor.

lessons.teachItBack: GitHub Copilot is strongest when it has the right IDE context. Ghost text handles fast completions, chat handles explanation, and agent mode handles multi-step changes. The lesson is to make context shaping and human review central to the workflow.

A prompting pattern that gives the model code before and after the cursor so it can complete the gap.

quiz.q: What should users do to help Copilot provide better IDE suggestions?
choices: Open relevant files and close irrelevant files
quiz.explanation: GitHub recommends shaping context by opening relevant files and closing irrelevant ones.

lessons.title: Arc / Dia — Browser as Context Engine
lessons.product: Arc / Dia by The Browser Company
lessons.summary: Arc Max and Dia show the browser becoming an AI context engine: page content, links, tabs, downloads, GSuite, Slack, and recent browsing all become usable work context.
contextSignals: Open tabs and browsing context in Dia
GSuite and Slack context in Dia when connected
lessons.analogy: A browser that stops being a pile of tabs and starts acting like an assistant who knows what is open on your desk.
lessons.diagram:
flowchart TD
  P[Current page] --> B[Browser context layer]
  L[Hovered link] --> B
  T[Tabs and sidebar] --> B
  D[Downloads] --> B
  W[GSuite, Slack, recent work in Dia] --> B
  B --> R{What does the user need?}
  R --> S[Summarize page or link]
  R --> O[Organize tabs/downloads]
  R --> Q[Answer from work context]
  R --> C[Command bar action]
  S --> U[Less tab switching]
  O --> U
  Q --> U
  C --> U
lessons.body:
## What it is
Arc Max was The Browser Company's first major AI bundle inside Arc. The official Arc help page describes features such as 5-second Previews, Tidy Tab Titles, Tidy Downloads, ChatGPT in the Command Bar, Instant Links, and Tidy Tabs. These are small, contextual AI actions embedded into browsing.

Dia is the larger browser-as-AI move. Dia's homepage says it digs into full context across GSuite, Slack, tabs, and more, and answers like someone who has seen every thread. The browser becomes the assistant's context container.

## Context signals it uses
Arc Max uses the page you are on, the link you hover, pinned tab titles, downloaded file contents, command-bar intent, and the current sidebar state. These are browser-native signals; the user does not have to copy text into a chatbot.

Dia expands the context surface to work apps and tabs. Its privacy page says Dia sometimes needs to share a little — such as your question and relevant context from the page you are on — to provide helpful answers. It also says browser data is encrypted and stored locally, with request data routed through servers to trusted AI partners.

## Patterns under the hood
Arc Max is mostly routing plus tool use. A hover can trigger a summarization route. A download can trigger a rename route. The command bar can route to ChatGPT or Instant Links. Dia is closer to RAG over browser and work context, retrieving relevant page, tab, Slack, or GSuite material for an answer.

Guardrails matter because browsers see sensitive things. The Dia privacy page is part of the product pattern: browser AI must explain what leaves the device, what is stored, and what users can turn off.

## UX & engagement moves
The brilliant Arc Max move is ambient AI. It does not demand that users become prompt engineers. Hover a link, press Shift, get a preview. Pin a tab, get a tidier title. Download a file, get a smarter name.

The command bar is the other major move. Browsers already have an omnibox habit. Arc and Dia use that habit as the launch surface for AI actions, reducing the friction of asking, searching, opening, and organizing.

## What to steal (lessons)
Put AI where the context already lives. In a browser, the best signals are pages, links, tabs, downloads, and app sessions. Do not ask users to paste what the browser can already see with permission.

But copy the privacy discipline too. Browser context is intimate. Explain what data is local, what is sent, who processes it, and what controls users have.

examples.what: Adds AI-powered previews, tab title cleanup, download renaming, ChatGPT in the command bar, Instant Links, and tab tidying directly in the browser.
Positions the browser as an assistant that can answer using full work context across tabs, GSuite, Slack, and more.

drills.q: What makes browser AI different from generic chat?
drills.a: The browser already has page, link, tab, download, and app context that can drive actions without copy-paste.

drills.q: What is the privacy risk of browser-as-context?
drills.a: The browser may see sensitive pages and work data, so users need clear controls and disclosure about what is sent or stored.

lessons.teachItBack: Arc Max starts with small contextual browser actions like link previews and tidy downloads. Dia pushes further by making tabs and work apps part of the assistant context. The lesson is to embed AI into existing browser habits while being explicit about privacy.

sources.title: Arc Help — Arc Max: Boost Your Browsing with AI
Dia — The browser for your best work
TechCrunch — Arc browser AI features combine OpenAI and Anthropic models

glossary.definition: AI behavior embedded into existing interactions so users get help without opening a separate assistant.
The pages, tabs, links, downloads, history, and connected web apps available to a browser-based assistant.

quiz.q: What is the strongest reusable idea from Arc Max?
choices: Make every feature a full chatbot
Trigger small AI actions from native browser context
quiz.explanation: Arc Max works by adding lightweight AI actions to browser-native signals like links, tabs, and downloads.

lessons.title: Raycast — Command Palette as AI Operating Layer
lessons.summary: Raycast turns the command launcher into an AI operating layer: selected text, clipboard, focused browser tab, Finder selection, extensions, and custom commands become one fast action surface.
contextSignals: Selected text in the frontmost app
Focused browser tab content via browser extension
Runtime arguments entered in the search bar
Extension environment, command name, mode, and launch type
Calendar or app data accessed through AI Extensions
lessons.analogy: A keyboard-first control tower: select something anywhere, hit the launcher, and dispatch the right AI or app command.
lessons.diagram:
flowchart LR
  K[Keyboard shortcut / Root Search] --> Q[Command query]
  S[Selected text] --> C[Context placeholders]
  B[Focused browser tab] --> C
  CL[Clipboard] --> C
  F[Finder selection] --> C
  Q --> R{Command route}
  C --> R
  R --> AI[AI Command]
  R --> EXT[Extension action]
  R --> APP[App or OS command]
  AI --> O[Rewrite, summarize, ask about page, find bugs]
  EXT --> O
  APP --> O
  O --> P[Paste, copy, show, or continue chat]
lessons.body:
## What it is
Raycast is a keyboard-first launcher and productivity platform. Its AI layer is interesting because it does not live in a separate AI app. AI Commands are just commands: pick text, open Raycast, run the command, get the result.

The official AI Commands manual says built-in commands include Improve Writing, Fix Spelling and Grammar, Explain This in Simple Terms, Find Bugs in Code, Summarize Webpage, and Ask About Webpage. Users can also create custom AI Commands with prompts, models, creativity settings, reasoning effort, tags, and dynamic placeholders.

## Context signals it uses
Raycast's context is operating-system context. Dynamic placeholders include clipboard, selected text from the frontmost app, arguments, date/time, and focused browser tab content when the browser extension is installed. The developer API can retrieve selected Finder items and selected text.

Extensions add tool context. Developers can build Raycast extensions with TypeScript, React, and Node, use Raycast UI components, and call the AI API without separate API keys for eligible users. That makes Raycast an orchestration layer across apps, files, selected text, web pages, and AI models.

## Patterns under the hood
Raycast is tool-use first. Every command is a tool, and AI Commands can call into extensions when a prompt needs more than text transformation. Routing happens through Root Search: the user types intent, Raycast ranks commands, and the selected command receives context.

Guardrails appear as permissions. Quick Fix needs Accessibility access to read and replace text in the active app. AI access can be checked through environment.canAccess. Context capture is useful because it is explicit and permissioned.

## UX & engagement moves
The command palette is the engagement move. It is fast, global, and keyboard-first. Users do not need to open a destination app, copy text, paste into chat, copy the answer, and paste back. The action starts where the user already is.

Raycast also uses progressive disclosure well. Simple users run built-in commands. Power users duplicate commands, add placeholders, choose models, and build extensions. The surface scales from "fix this sentence" to "make a custom workflow over my tools."

## What to steal (lessons)
Make AI available at the point of selection. If the user already highlighted text, selected files, or focused a web tab, that is your context. Do not ask them to restate it.

Also make custom commands first-class. Repeated prompts are workflows in disguise. Turn them into named, keyboard-accessible actions with arguments and placeholders.

examples.what: Turns repeated prompts into commands that can use selected text, focused fields, browser tab content, arguments, model settings, and AI Extensions.
Injects clipboard, selected text, focused browser tab, arguments, date/time, and other live context into commands.

drills.q: Why does Raycast make AI feel fast?
drills.a: It launches from the keyboard and uses already-selected or focused context instead of requiring copy-paste into a separate chat app.

drills.q: What is a repeated prompt in Raycast's product model?
drills.a: A candidate AI Command: a named, reusable action with placeholders, arguments, and model settings.

lessons.teachItBack: Raycast makes AI feel like an operating-system command. It can use selected text, clipboard, focused browser tabs, files, and extensions as context. The product lesson is to turn common prompts into fast, permissioned, keyboard-accessible workflows.

glossary.definition: A reusable Raycast command backed by a prompt, optional placeholders, model settings, and actions over selected or focused context.
A token that injects live context such as selected text, clipboard, arguments, or browser tab content into a command.

quiz.q: What is Raycast's key AI UX advantage?
choices: It only works inside one document
It uses global command-palette context like selection, clipboard, and browser tab
quiz.explanation: Raycast brings AI to the active app and selected context through a fast command palette.

lessons.title: Notion AI — Workspace Knowledge as Context
lessons.summary: Notion AI works because the workspace is already structured context: pages, databases, wikis, projects, meeting notes, permissions, embeddings, and inline editing all feed the assistant.
contextSignals: Highlighted text or cursor location in a page
Workspace pages and databases the user can access
Project roadmaps, tasks, meeting notes, docs, and wikis
Connected apps and web context for some Notion AI features
Permission model determining which pages can be referenced
lessons.analogy: A teammate who has read the company wiki, sprint board, meeting notes, and docs — but only the pages they are allowed to see.
lessons.diagram:
flowchart TD
  W[Workspace pages, databases, docs, wikis] --> E[Embeddings / semantic index]
  P[User permissions] --> F[Accessible content filter]
  E --> F
  Q[Q&A question or inline request] --> R{Need workspace search?}
  R -->|yes| F
  F --> K[Relevant pages ranked]
  K --> A[Answer with workspace context]
  R -->|no| G[Draft, edit, summarize current page]
  A --> S[Sources / referenced pages]
  G --> U[Inline document update]
  S --> U
lessons.body:
## What it is
Notion AI is embedded into the workspace where teams already keep docs, projects, databases, meeting notes, and wikis. Q&A lets users ask questions across that workspace and get answers from pages they can access. Writer and inline AI help draft, edit, summarize, translate, and brainstorm inside pages.

The product advantage is that Notion already has structured work context. The user does not need to build a knowledge base for AI; the workspace is the knowledge base.

## Context signals it uses
Notion's Q&A blog says Q&A uses information across knowledge, docs, projects, meeting notes, and more. It can be opened from Search, the sparkle icon, or a keyboard shortcut, and it only references pages the user has permission to view.

The security practices page gives the architectural version: Notion generates embeddings from workspace content, stores them in a vector database, uses a query to find relevant pages, refines and ranks pages by relevance, and generates responses informed by those pages. Inline Writer uses the current page context for drafting, editing, and summarization.

## Patterns under the hood
Notion AI is workspace RAG. The retrieval corpus is not the public web; it is your company's documents and databases. Long-term memory is the workspace itself. Routing decides whether the request needs Q&A over the workspace, inline writing help, database Autofill, meeting notes, or web/model knowledge.

Guardrails come from permissions and data handling. Notion states that Q&A references only information in pages the user can view, and that customer data is not used to train AI models in the Q&A launch post.

## UX & engagement moves
The inline move is the key. Users can hit space in a page and ask AI to draft, edit, summarize, or transform without leaving the document. Q&A also appears where search already lives, so asking the workspace feels like a natural extension of finding a page.

Trust comes from source and permission expectations. In a company workspace, answers are only useful if people believe they are grounded in the right docs and do not leak restricted pages. The product's permission model becomes part of the UX.

## What to steal (lessons)
If users already store knowledge in your product, make that structure the AI context layer. Pages, databases, tasks, owners, timestamps, and permissions are all signals.

Do not separate AI from work. Put writing help inside the editor, Q&A inside search, and summaries inside databases. The best AI surface is often the place where the work object already lives.

examples.what: Answers questions using information across workspace knowledge, docs, projects, meeting notes, and pages the user has permission to view.
Explains embeddings, vector search, page retrieval, relevance ranking, and response generation over workspace content.

drills.q: Why is Notion AI a strong RAG example?
drills.a: Its retrieval corpus is the user's structured workspace: docs, databases, projects, meeting notes, and wikis.

drills.q: What product constraint makes workspace Q&A trustworthy?
drills.a: It must respect page permissions and show or imply which workspace content informed the answer.

lessons.teachItBack: Notion AI uses the workspace as its memory. Q&A retrieves across pages and databases, while inline AI helps draft and edit where work already happens. The lesson is to turn existing structured content and permissions into the AI context layer.
