lessons.title: The Agentic System-Design Framework
lessons.summary: A repeatable six-step method — Requirements → Constraints & Budgets → High-Level Design → Deep Dives → Trade-offs → Review — adapted from classic system-design interviews to designing AI agents.
lessons.analogy: An architect doesn't start by pouring concrete. They ask what the building is for, survey the land and budget, sketch the floor plan, then detail the load-bearing walls. System design is the same discipline pointed at software.
lessons.body:
## First principles
Every system-design interview question (Hello Interview, Grokking, the System Design Primer) is really one question: **given fuzzy requirements and hard constraints, can you propose a structure that works, and reason about its trade-offs?** The structure matters more than any single 'right' answer.

The trap is jumping straight to boxes-and-arrows before you know what you're building or what limits you. A framework forces you to *earn* the architecture: requirements justify the design, constraints size it, and trade-offs defend it.

## Plain definition
A system-design framework is a fixed **sequence of questions** you walk through, in order, so you never skip a step under pressure. It turns an open-ended 'design X' into a checklist.

## The six steps (classic → agentic)
Classic interviews use roughly: clarify requirements → estimate scale → high-level design → deep dive → trade-offs → wrap up. Agents add new concerns, so each step grows:

1. **Requirements** — functional (what actions) + non-functional (latency, safety, autonomy). *Agentic add: what can it sense, how autonomous, what's the success metric.*
2. **Constraints & Budgets** — back-of-envelope numbers. *Agentic add: latency budget tied to human perception, cost-per-task, token/context limits, on-device vs cloud.*
3. **High-Level Design** — the block diagram. *Agentic add: Sensors → Context Engine → Planner → Tools → Guardrails → Memory → UI.*
4. **Deep Dives** — zoom into the risky component. *Agentic add: the sensing→grounding pipeline, the planner/policy, the eval harness.*
5. **Trade-offs** — name what you gave up. *Agentic add: autonomy vs safety, on-device vs capability, latency vs quality.*
6. **Review** — does the design meet every requirement? Walk a request end-to-end. *Agentic add: failure-mode and eval review.*

## Why agents need the extra concerns
A web service fails by being slow or down. An **agent** fails by *doing the wrong thing confidently* — clicking the wrong button, leaking a screen, looping forever. So the framework bolts on four agent-specific lenses everywhere: **context/sensing, tools/actions, evals, and guardrails**.

## How to use this lane
The next five lessons walk each step in depth, then two **blueprint** lessons apply the whole method to design a context- and platform-aware desktop assistant. Learn the method once; reuse it on any agent.
lessons.diagram:
flowchart LR
  R[1. Requirements] --> C[2. Constraints & Budgets]
  C --> H[3. High-Level Design]
  H --> D[4. Deep Dives]
  D --> T[5. Trade-offs]
  T --> V[6. Review]
  V -. iterate .-> R
  subgraph Agentic lenses applied at every step
    L1[Context / Sensing]
    L2[Tools / Actions]
    L3[Evals]
    L4[Guardrails]
  end

examples.what: Teaches the 'in a hurry' delivery framework: requirements, core entities, API, high-level design, deep dives — the backbone we adapt for agents.
Argues you should start simple and only add agentic complexity (loops, tools, autonomy) when requirements justify it — the trade-off mindset this framework enforces.

drills.q: Why walk the steps in order instead of jumping to the architecture diagram?
drills.a: Because requirements justify the design and constraints size it. Drawing boxes first means you can't defend why they exist or whether they fit the budget.

drills.q: What four agent-specific lenses does this framework add to the classic method?
drills.a: Context/sensing, tools/actions, evals, and guardrails — applied at every step, because agents fail by acting wrongly, not just by being slow.

drills.q: How does an agent's failure mode differ from a typical web service's?
drills.a: A service fails by being slow or unavailable; an agent fails by confidently doing the wrong thing — wrong action, privacy leak, or infinite loop.

lessons.teachItBack: Agentic system design is the classic six-step method — requirements, constraints, high-level design, deep dives, trade-offs, review — with four extra lenses for sensing, tools, evals, and guardrails. You walk the steps in order so requirements justify the architecture and budgets size it. The payoff is that you can defend every box and name what you traded away.

lessons.title: Step 1 — Requirements for an Agent
lessons.summary: How to nail down what an agent must do before designing it: separate functional from non-functional requirements and ask the five clarifying questions — who, what actions, autonomy level, success metric, and safety bounds.
lessons.analogy: A good contractor won't build until they know who lives here, what rooms you need, and what you absolutely cannot live without. Requirements are that interview — done badly, you build the wrong house perfectly.
lessons.body:
## First principles
Design problems are deliberately vague. 'Build an agent that helps the user' could mean a thousand systems. The first job is **compression of ambiguity into a contract**: a short list of what the system must do (functional) and how well it must do it (non-functional). Everything downstream — budgets, architecture, evals — derives from this contract.

## Plain definition
- **Functional requirements (FRs)** = the *actions* the system performs. Verbs. 'Summarize the active document', 'click the Send button', 'search the user's files'.
- **Non-functional requirements (NFRs)** = the *qualities* those actions must have. Adjectives. Fast, cheap, safe, private, autonomous-but-reversible.

For agents the NFRs are where the design lives — latency, autonomy, and safety shape the whole architecture far more than the feature list.

## The five clarifying questions for any agent
Ask these before drawing a single box:

1. **WHO** is the user and what's their environment? (desktop power-user vs phone novice; one app vs the whole OS.)
2. **WHAT ACTIONS** may it take? (read-only suggestions? click/type? spend money?) This sets the blast radius.
3. **AUTONOMY LEVEL** — suggest, confirm-then-act, or act-then-report? (Anthropic: prefer the least autonomy that solves the task.)
4. **SUCCESS METRIC** — how do we know it worked? (task completion rate, time saved, user acceptance of suggestions.)
5. **SAFETY / BOUNDS** — what must it never do? (no irreversible actions without confirmation, no reading password fields, no exfiltrating the screen.)

## Scoping: in vs out
State explicitly what's **out of scope**. 'V1 reads screen + cursor and *suggests* one action; it does not auto-execute or touch other apps.' Narrow scope is a feature — it shrinks the budget, the attack surface, and the eval set.

## Turning answers into a contract
Write FRs and NFRs as testable bullets. 'FR: given a selected paragraph, propose a rewrite.' 'NFR: suggestion appears within 300 ms of selection (perceptible-instant budget).' 'NFR: never act without one click of confirmation in V1.' Each becomes an eval later.

## Watch out for
- **Gold-plating**: don't promise full autonomy when confirm-then-act meets the metric.
- **Hidden NFRs**: privacy and reversibility are easy to forget and expensive to retrofit.
- **Unmeasurable goals**: 'be helpful' isn't a metric; 'user accepts ≥60% of suggestions' is.
lessons.diagram:
flowchart TD
  Q[Vague prompt: 'design a helpful agent'] --> C{Clarify}
  C --> W1[WHO + environment]
  C --> W2[WHAT actions / blast radius]
  C --> W3[AUTONOMY level]
  C --> W4[SUCCESS metric]
  C --> W5[SAFETY bounds]
  W1 & W2 & W3 & W4 & W5 --> FR[Functional reqs: verbs]
  W1 & W2 & W3 & W4 & W5 --> NFR[Non-functional reqs: qualities]
  FR --> CT[Testable contract]
  NFR --> CT

examples.what: Scoped its first release to a confirm-heavy browser agent that pauses for the user on sensitive actions (logins, payments) — an explicit autonomy + safety requirement, not an afterthought.
Shipped as a capability with loud safety caveats and a recommendation to constrain actions — illustrating that 'what actions may it take' is a primary requirement.

drills.q: What is the difference between a functional and a non-functional requirement?
drills.a: Functional = the actions the system performs (verbs). Non-functional = the qualities those actions must have (fast, safe, private, autonomous). For agents, NFRs drive most of the design.

drills.q: Why is 'what actions may it take' the most safety-relevant clarifying question?
drills.a: Because it sets the blast radius — read-only suggestions are low-risk, while clicking, typing, or spending money makes mistakes consequential and demands guardrails.

drills.q: Rewrite the vague goal 'be helpful' as a testable success metric.
drills.a: e.g. 'The user accepts at least 60% of proposed actions' or 'median task completed in under 30s with one confirmation' — something you can measure in an eval.

lessons.teachItBack: Before designing an agent, turn the vague prompt into a contract of functional requirements (the actions) and non-functional requirements (the qualities like latency, autonomy, and safety). Get there by asking five clarifying questions: who, what actions, autonomy level, success metric, and safety bounds. Then write each as a testable bullet that later becomes an eval.

How much an agent acts on its own: suggest-only, confirm-then-act, or act-then-report. Lower autonomy = lower risk.
The scope of harm an agent's actions can cause; widened by write/click/spend permissions, narrowed by read-only scoping.

quiz.q: Which clarifying question most directly determines an agent's safety blast radius?
quiz.explanation: The set of permitted actions (read vs click vs spend) defines how much harm a mistake can cause.

quiz.q: For agents, where does most of the architectural pressure come from?
quiz.explanation: NFRs like latency, autonomy, and safety shape the architecture far more than the feature list.

lessons.title: Step 2 — Constraints & Budgets
lessons.summary: Turn requirements into numbers: latency budgets tied to human perception limits, cost-per-task, token/context limits, and privacy/on-device constraints — the back-of-the-envelope math that sizes an agent.
lessons.analogy: A budget is a fence around your design space. Before you decorate a room you measure the walls; before you architect an agent you measure your milliseconds, dollars, and tokens.
lessons.body:
## First principles
Constraints are what make design *engineering* rather than wishful thinking. Two designs can both 'work' on paper; the one that fits the latency, cost, and token budgets is the real one. Estimation isn't about precision — it's about finding the **order of magnitude** that rules architectures in or out.

## Latency budget — anchor it to human perception
Nielsen Norman Group's three response-time limits are the canonical anchors:
- **0.1 s (100 ms)** — feels *instant*. Direct-manipulation feedback (a hover hint, a cursor-anchored suggestion) must land here.
- **1 s** — keeps the user's *flow of thought* unbroken. A short agent suggestion should resolve by ~1 s.
- **10 s** — the limit of *keeping attention*. Beyond this, stream partial results or show progress or the user leaves.

So: cheap local UI cues ≤100 ms; a model suggestion target ~1 s; any multi-step tool action must **stream** (see streaming-responses) to survive past 10 s. These numbers dictate where you put caching, on-device models, and prefetching.

## Decompose the latency
A single agent suggestion is a chain. Budget each hop:

`sense (capture screen/cursor) + ground (build context) + model (LLM call) + tool (act) + render`

If your total budget is 1 s and the LLM round-trip alone is 700 ms, sensing+grounding must be cheap (a11y tree read, not full OCR) and you must prefetch/predict. This is the math that forces on-device vs cloud splits.

## Cost-per-task
Estimate: `tokens_in + tokens_out` per step × steps per task × price/token. If a task is 5 ReAct steps × ~3k tokens × $X, you get a per-task cost. Multiply by daily active tasks → infra bill. High cost pushes you toward smaller models, caching, and fewer loop iterations.

## Token / context limits
Context windows are finite. A screen-reading agent could capture *megabytes* of accessibility tree + OCR text. You **cannot** dump it all in. Budget tokens: reserve room for system prompt, tools, and reply; the rest is your context budget. This is why the context pipeline (Step 4) must rank and compress.

## Privacy / on-device constraints
Some data must never leave the machine (screen contents, keystrokes). That's a hard constraint, not a preference. It forces: on-device capture, on-device redaction before any cloud call, and possibly an on-device small model for sensitive paths. Privacy budgets are as real as latency budgets.

## Back-of-the-envelope, worked
- Perception-instant cue: **≤100 ms** → must be local, no network.
- Model suggestion: **~1 s** → one cloud LLM call, small model, prefetched context.
- Context budget: window 200k tokens − 8k (system+tools) − 4k (reply) ≈ **188k** usable, but aim for ~4–8k of *ranked* context for latency/cost, not the max.
- Cost: 5 steps × 3k tokens ≈ 15k tokens/task → at scale, the line item that decides your model tier.

## Watch out for
- **Latency tail, not mean**: the p95 is what users feel; budget for it.
- **Token bloat**: raw screen captures explode context; compress early.
- **Forgotten privacy cost**: redaction and on-device inference add latency you must pre-budget.
lessons.diagram:
flowchart LR
  subgraph Latency budget ~1s
    S[Sense ≤150ms] --> G[Ground/context ≤150ms] --> M[Model call ~600ms] --> A[Tool/act ≤50ms] --> R[Render ≤50ms]
  end
  P[NN/g limits: 100ms instant · 1s flow · 10s attention] -.anchors.-> S
  B[Token budget: window − system − reply = usable] -.caps.-> G
  C[Cost: tokens × steps × price] -.bounds.-> M

examples.what: The 0.1s / 1s / 10s thresholds are the industry anchors for what 'fast enough' means; agent UIs inherit them directly.
Demos show multi-second screenshot→reason→click loops, which is exactly why such agents must stream progress and stay under the 10s attention limit per step.

drills.q: What are NN/g's three response-time limits and what does each govern?
drills.a: 0.1s = feels instant (direct feedback); 1s = unbroken flow of thought; 10s = limit of attention. Past 10s you must stream or show progress.

drills.q: Your suggestion latency budget is 1s and the LLM call is 700ms. What does that force in the rest of the design?
drills.a: Sensing and context-building must be cheap (read the a11y tree, not full OCR), and you must prefetch/predict context so only ~300ms is left for everything else.

drills.q: Why can't a screen-reading agent put its full captured context into the model?
drills.a: Raw a11y tree + OCR can be megabytes; the context window is finite and tokens cost latency and money, so the context must be ranked and compressed first.

lessons.teachItBack: Step two turns requirements into numbers: anchor latency to NN/g's 100ms/1s/10s perception limits, decompose the budget across sense-ground-model-act-render, then estimate cost-per-task and your token budget. Hard privacy constraints can force on-device capture and redaction before any cloud call. These numbers rule whole architectures in or out before you draw them.

lessons.title: Step 3 — High-Level Design
lessons.summary: The reference architecture for a context- and platform-aware agent: Sensors → Context Engine → Planner/Policy → Tool/Action layer → Guardrails → Memory → UI. One diagram you can adapt to almost any agent.
lessons.analogy: Like the human nervous system: senses gather signals, the brain builds a picture and decides, muscles act, and reflexes (guardrails) stop you from touching the hot stove. Each agent component maps to one of these roles.
lessons.body:
## First principles
Once you have requirements and budgets, the high-level design is about **naming the boxes and the arrows between them** — the data flow from perceiving the world to acting on it. A good reference architecture is reusable: the same seven blocks describe a customer-support bot or a screen-aware desktop assistant; only the implementations change.

The core loop of *any* agent is **perceive → decide → act**, wrapped with memory and safety. Our reference architecture just expands that loop into named, swappable components.

## The seven blocks
1. **Sensors** — gather raw signals about the user and environment. For a desktop agent: cursor position, selection/hover, active app (accessibility tree), screen (screenshot + OCR), recent history, OS capabilities.
2. **Context Engine (Context Builder)** — normalize, rank, and compress those signals into a compact, model-ready context. This is where 'too much raw data' becomes 'the few facts that matter now.'
3. **Planner / Policy** — the decision-maker. Given the context and the goal, decide *what to do*: which tool, which UI target, what search query. May be a single LLM call, a ReAct loop, or a planner-executor.
4. **Tool / Action layer** — the hands. Typed tools the planner can invoke: search, click(target), type(text), open(app), call an API. Each tool has a schema and a permission.
5. **Guardrails** — the safety reflex wrapping the action layer: validate the proposed action, check permissions, require confirmation for risky/irreversible ops, redact sensitive data.
6. **Memory** — short-term (this session's context) and long-term (user preferences, past actions, learned shortcuts). Feeds the context engine and personalizes the planner.
7. **UI** — how the agent surfaces suggestions and asks for confirmation: cursor-anchored hints, a side panel, streaming text. This is where UX patterns (microinteractions, motion, streaming) live.

## How a request flows
Sensors fire → Context Engine builds a ranked context → Planner decides an action + target + query → Guardrails check it → Tool layer executes (or UI asks the user first) → result and action are written to Memory → UI renders the outcome. Then the loop repeats on the next signal.

## Where to put the model(s)
You rarely use one model everywhere. A tiny on-device model can rank context and gate privacy; a larger cloud model does the hard planning. The architecture lets you slot different models into the Context Engine vs the Planner based on the latency/cost/privacy budgets from Step 2.

## Map it to the budgets
- Sensors + Context Engine must fit the ≤150ms sensing budget → cheap, often on-device.
- Planner is the expensive hop → the ~600ms model call.
- Guardrails + Tools are cheap but safety-critical → fast checks, human-in-the-loop on risk.

## Watch out for
- **Skipping the Context Engine**: dumping raw sensors into the planner blows the token budget and the latency budget.
- **Guardrails as an afterthought**: bolt them around the action layer from day one.
- **No memory**: without it the agent re-asks and never learns the user's patterns.

    SEN[Sensors\ncursor · selection · a11y tree · screenshot+OCR · history · OS caps]
    CE[Context Engine\nnormalize · rank · compress]
    SEN --> CE
  end
  subgraph Decide
    PL[Planner / Policy\nwhat tool · which target · what query]
    MEM[(Memory\nshort + long term)]
    CE --> PL
    MEM <--> PL

  end
  subgraph Act

examples.what: Maps cleanly onto this: it senses the browser (screenshots), a model plans the next action, an action layer clicks/types, and guardrails pause for user confirmation on sensitive steps.
Describes agents as a planner plus tools plus memory operating in an environment — the same perceive-decide-act decomposition this reference architecture formalizes.

drills.q: What are the seven blocks of the reference architecture, in data-flow order?
drills.a: Sensors → Context Engine → Planner/Policy → Guardrails → Tool/Action layer → (writes to) Memory → UI, with Memory feeding back into Context Engine and Planner.

drills.q: Why is the Context Engine a separate block instead of feeding sensors straight to the planner?
drills.a: Raw sensor data is too large and noisy; the Context Engine normalizes, ranks, and compresses it to fit the token and latency budgets and to surface only what matters now.

drills.q: Where do guardrails sit and why there?
drills.a: Wrapped around the Tool/Action layer, between the planner's decision and real execution, so every proposed action is validated, permission-checked, and (if risky) confirmed before it touches the world.

lessons.teachItBack: The reference architecture is seven blocks expanding the perceive-decide-act loop: Sensors gather signals, the Context Engine compresses them, the Planner decides the action/target/query, Guardrails check it, the Tool layer executes, Memory records and personalizes, and the UI surfaces it. A request flows straight through these blocks and the loop repeats on the next signal. You slot different models and budgets into each block, keeping sensing cheap and the planner as the expensive hop.

glossary.definition: A reusable block-and-arrow template (here: Sensors → Context Engine → Planner → Guardrails → Tools → Memory → UI) adaptable across many agents.
The component that normalizes, ranks, and compresses raw sensor signals into compact, model-ready context.
The decision-making component that maps context + goal to an action, a target, and any query parameters.

quiz.q: In the reference architecture, what is the Context Engine's job?
choices: Normalize, rank, and compress raw sensor signals into model-ready context
quiz.explanation: The Context Engine turns noisy, oversized sensor data into the few compact facts that matter for the current decision.

quiz.q: Which placement of guardrails is correct?
choices: Wrapped around the tool/action layer, after the planner decides
As a post-hoc log reviewed weekly
quiz.explanation: Guardrails must validate and gate the action between the planner's decision and real execution.

lessons.title: Step 4 — Deep Dive: The Context Pipeline
lessons.summary: A deep dive on the sensing→grounding pipeline: capture raw signals (cursor, selection, screen, accessibility tree), normalize them, rank and compress into a context, then ground the user's intent to a concrete UI target and a search query.
lessons.analogy: A great assistant glances at your desk, notices what you're pointing at, ignores the clutter, and hands you the one document you reached for — before you finish the sentence. The context pipeline is that glance, formalized.
lessons.body:
## First principles
The hardest part of a context-aware agent isn't the model — it's **feeding it the right context**. Garbage context produces confident wrong actions. The deep dive picks the riskiest component from the high-level design (the Context Engine) and details its internal stages. Goal: turn a flood of raw signals into a few grounded facts the planner can act on, within the ≤150ms sensing budget.

## The five stages
### 1. Capture
Pull raw signals from the OS and apps:
- **Cursor position** (x,y) and velocity — where attention is.
- **Selection / hover** — the exact text or element under focus.
- **Active app + window** and its **accessibility (a11y) tree** — a structured list of UI elements (buttons, fields, labels) with roles and bounds. This is the cheap, high-signal source.
- **Screen** via screenshot + **OCR** — the fallback when no a11y tree exists (canvas apps, images).
- **History** — recent actions, clipboard, open files.

### 2. Normalize
Different sources speak different languages. Convert everything to a common schema: `{element, role, text, bounds, source, confidence, timestamp}`. Resolve the element under the cursor by hit-testing the a11y tree against the cursor (x,y). De-duplicate signals that describe the same thing.

### 3. Rank
You can't send it all. Score each candidate by **relevance to likely intent**: proximity to the cursor, recency, focus/selection state, and semantic match to the user's apparent goal. The element under the cursor and the current selection get top weight; distant chrome gets dropped.

### 4. Compress
Fit the ranked context into the token budget. Summarize long text, keep structured fields, drop low-confidence noise. Aim for a few thousand tokens of *signal*, not the megabytes of raw capture. Redact sensitive fields here (passwords, tokens) before anything leaves the device.

### 5. Ground
The payoff stage: map fuzzy intent to **concrete handles** the action layer can use:
- **UI target** — a stable selector for the element to act on (a11y path / role+name, not raw pixels, so it survives layout changes).
- **Search query** — the precise string to look up, derived from the selection + surrounding context (e.g. selected error text → 'how to fix <error>').
- **Intent label** — what the user probably wants (define, summarize, fix, navigate).

Grounding is what lets the agent 'know where to go and what to search' — it's the bridge from perception to a callable action.

## Worked micro-example
User hovers a red error string in an IDE.
- Capture: cursor over a label, a11y role=staticText, text='TypeError: undefined is not a function', active app=VS Code.
- Normalize: one element, high confidence, IDE source.
- Rank: cursor-anchored + selected → top.
- Compress: keep the error text + file context, drop the sidebar.
- Ground: intent='fix error'; search query='TypeError undefined is not a function javascript'; UI target='Quick Fix lightbulb at bounds'.

The planner now has everything to act in one cheap pass.

## Watch out for
- **Pixel-only targeting** is brittle; prefer a11y selectors so actions survive re-layout.
- **Over-capturing**: full screenshots every frame blow latency and privacy budgets — prefer the a11y tree, OCR only on demand.
- **Stale context**: timestamp signals and discard old ones; the cursor moved.
- **No confidence scores**: without them you can't rank or know when to ask the user.
lessons.diagram:
flowchart LR
  subgraph Capture
    CUR[Cursor x,y + velocity]
    SEL[Selection / hover]
    A11Y[Accessibility tree]
    SCR[Screenshot + OCR]
    HIS[History / clipboard]
  end
  CUR & SEL & A11Y & SCR & HIS --> N[Normalize\ncommon schema + hit-test cursor]
  N --> RK[Rank\nproximity · recency · focus · semantic match]
  RK --> CMP[Compress\nsummarize · redact · fit token budget]
  CMP --> GRD[Ground]
  GRD --> T1[UI target\na11y selector]
  GRD --> T2[Search query]
  GRD --> T3[Intent label]
  T1 & T2 & T3 --> PLN([To Planner])

examples.what: Grounds actions from screenshots — the model locates on-screen targets and emits coordinates/clicks, illustrating the capture→ground path (and why pixel targeting is brittle).
Builds context from rendered pages and grounds intent to specific page elements and queries before acting — a productized capture→rank→ground pipeline.

drills.q: What are the five stages of the context pipeline?
drills.a: Capture (raw signals) → Normalize (common schema, hit-test the cursor) → Rank (by relevance to intent) → Compress (fit token budget, redact) → Ground (to a UI target, search query, and intent label).

drills.q: Why prefer an accessibility-tree selector over raw pixel coordinates for a UI target?
drills.a: Because a11y selectors (role + name / path) are stable across layout, resolution, and theme changes, while pixel coordinates break the moment the UI shifts.

drills.q: What does the 'ground' stage produce, and why is it the key to acting on the user's behalf?
drills.a: It produces a concrete UI target, a precise search query, and an intent label — the callable handles that let the agent know exactly where to go and what to search.

lessons.teachItBack: The context pipeline turns a flood of signals into a few grounded facts in five stages: capture cursor/selection/a11y tree/screen, normalize to one schema, rank by relevance to intent, compress to fit the token budget while redacting secrets, and ground intent into a concrete UI target plus a search query. Prefer the accessibility tree over screenshots for speed and stability, and prefer a11y selectors over pixels so actions survive re-layout. Grounding is what lets the agent know where to go and what to search.

glossary.definition: A structured, machine-readable list of an app's UI elements with roles, names, and bounds — exposed by the OS for assistive tech and ideal as a cheap, stable sensing source.
Mapping fuzzy user intent to concrete, callable handles: a UI target selector, a search query, and an intent label.
Determining which UI element lies under a given cursor coordinate by matching the point against element bounds.

quiz.q: What is the output of the 'ground' stage of the context pipeline?
choices: A concrete UI target, a search query, and an intent label
A list of every open window
quiz.explanation: Grounding produces the callable handles — UI target, search query, intent — the planner needs to act.

quiz.q: Why is the accessibility tree usually preferred over screenshot+OCR for sensing?
choices: It is structured, cheaper, and more stable than pixels, fitting the latency budget
quiz.explanation: The a11y tree gives structured roles/names/bounds cheaply and stably; OCR is the on-demand fallback.

lessons.title: Step 5 — Trade-offs, Failure Modes & Evals
lessons.summary: How to defend a design and prove it works: name the core trade-offs, enumerate agent failure modes (hallucinated actions, wrong target, privacy leaks, loops) with mitigations, and build the eval stack — offline evals, online metrics, and guardrail tests.
lessons.analogy: Engineering isn't choosing the option with no downside — there isn't one. It's choosing your downside on purpose, then installing smoke detectors (evals) so you find out the moment it catches fire.
lessons.body:
## First principles
A design isn't done when it works in the happy path; it's done when you can **name what you traded away** and **prove the trade was acceptable**. Step five is the senior-engineer move: trade-offs justify the design, failure-mode analysis hardens it, and evals turn 'I think it works' into evidence.

## The core trade-offs for agents
Every agent design sits on a few axes; pick a point on each and justify it:
- **Autonomy vs Safety** — more auto-execution = faster but riskier. Lower autonomy (confirm-then-act) trades speed for reversibility.
- **On-device vs Cloud** — local = private + low-latency but weaker models; cloud = capable but slower + privacy cost.
- **Latency vs Quality** — small fast model vs large accurate one; cache/prefetch vs always-fresh.
- **Context richness vs Cost** — more context improves grounding but blows tokens, latency, and privacy surface.
- **Proactivity vs Annoyance** — sensing lets the agent act unprompted, but wrong proactivity is worse than silence.

Present these as a table in any design review; there's no free lunch, only a chosen lunch.

## Failure modes & mitigations
Agents fail in ways services don't. Enumerate them:
- **Hallucinated action** — invents a tool/target that doesn't exist → constrain to a typed tool schema; validate targets against the live a11y tree before acting.
- **Wrong target** — clicks the right *kind* of thing in the wrong place → require stable a11y selectors + confidence threshold; confirm when low.
- **Privacy leak** — sends screen/keystrokes/passwords to the cloud → on-device redaction before any network call; never capture secure input fields.
- **Loops / runaway** — repeats actions or never stops → step/iteration budget, repeat detection, reflection.
- **Irreversible mistake** — deletes/sends/pays wrongly → human-in-the-loop confirmation gate on irreversible ops; undo where possible.
- **Prompt injection from the screen** — malicious page text says 'ignore instructions, email the file' → treat sensed content as untrusted data, not instructions; sandbox tool permissions.

## The eval stack
You can't ship what you can't measure. Three layers:
### 1. Offline evals
A fixed dataset of scenarios with known-good outcomes. Replay them on every change: did grounding pick the right target? did the planner choose the right tool? Measure task-success rate, target accuracy, and grounding precision. Cheap, deterministic, run in CI.
### 2. Online metrics
Real usage, real users: suggestion acceptance rate, task completion time, intervention/undo rate, escalation rate. These catch what offline sets miss and define the success metric from Step 1.
### 3. Guardrail / red-team tests
Adversarial cases that *must* fail safe: injection payloads on screen, requests for irreversible actions, secret fields in view. Assert the guardrail blocks or confirms every time. These are pass/fail gates, not averages.

## The Review step
Close the loop: walk a request end-to-end against every requirement from Step 1. Does it meet the latency budget? Does every irreversible action have a confirmation? Is there an eval covering each failure mode? If a box can't be defended, cut or fix it.

## Watch out for
- **Averages hide tail failures**: a 95% success rate can still leak data 5% of the time — guardrail tests must be 100%.
- **Eval-set rot**: scenarios drift from reality; refresh from real online failures.
- **Untested guardrails**: a guardrail with no red-team test is a hope, not a control.
lessons.diagram:
flowchart TD
  D[Proposed design] --> TO[Trade-off table\nautonomy·on-device·latency·context·proactivity]
  D --> FM[Failure-mode list\nhallucinated action · wrong target · leak · loop · injection]
  FM --> MIT[Mitigations\ntyped tools · a11y validation · redaction · budgets · HITL]
  subgraph Eval stack
    OFF[Offline evals\nfixed scenarios · CI]
    ON[Online metrics\nacceptance · time · undo rate]
    RT[Guardrail / red-team\nmust fail safe]
  end
  MIT --> OFF --> ON
  MIT --> RT
  OFF & ON & RT --> REV{Review\nmeets all reqs?}
  REV -- no --> D
  REV -- yes --> SHIP([Ship])

examples.what: Advocates starting simple, adding autonomy only when justified, and measuring — the exact trade-off-and-evaluate discipline of this step.
Ships with confirmation gates on sensitive actions and published safety evaluations/red-teaming — a real example of guardrail tests and human-in-the-loop as design requirements.

drills.q: Name three core trade-off axes for an agent design and the tension on each.
drills.a: Autonomy vs safety (speed vs reversibility); on-device vs cloud (privacy/latency vs capability); latency vs quality (small fast model vs large accurate one). Also context richness vs cost, proactivity vs annoyance.

drills.q: Why must guardrail/red-team tests be pass/fail rather than averaged?
drills.a: Because a privacy leak or irreversible mistake even 1% of the time is unacceptable; safety controls must hold 100% of the time, unlike quality metrics that can be averaged.

drills.q: How should an agent treat text it reads off the screen, and why?
drills.a: As untrusted data, never as instructions — otherwise malicious on-screen text can prompt-inject the agent into harmful actions. Sandbox tool permissions to limit the blast radius.

lessons.teachItBack: Step five defends and proves the design: lay out the trade-off axes (autonomy vs safety, on-device vs cloud, latency vs quality) and pick a point on each deliberately. Enumerate agent failure modes — hallucinated actions, wrong targets, privacy leaks, loops, prompt injection — and attach a mitigation to each. Then build the eval stack: offline scenario evals in CI, online metrics like acceptance and undo rate, and pass/fail red-team guardrail tests, and review the whole thing end-to-end against the original requirements.

glossary.definition: Replaying a fixed dataset of scenarios with known-good outcomes to measure agent quality deterministically, typically in CI.
A design where the agent pauses for explicit user confirmation before risky or irreversible actions.
An attack where malicious text in the agent's input (e.g. on-screen content) tries to override its instructions; mitigated by treating sensed content as untrusted data.

quiz.q: Why must guardrail/red-team tests be treated as pass/fail gates rather than averaged metrics?
choices: A safety failure even a small percent of the time is unacceptable
quiz.explanation: Privacy leaks and irreversible mistakes must never happen, so safety controls must hold 100% of the time.

quiz.q: How should an agent treat instructions found in text it reads off the user's screen?
choices: As untrusted data, never as instructions
As higher priority than the user's request
quiz.explanation: Sensed content can be a prompt-injection vector, so it must be treated as untrusted data with sandboxed tool permissions.

lessons.title: Blueprint — The Cursor/Context-Aware Desktop Agent
lessons.summary: The capstone: a complete end-to-end design for a context- and platform-aware desktop assistant that senses your screen, app state, and mouse cursor, then figures out where to go and what to search to act on your behalf — built by applying the whole framework.
lessons.analogy: Imagine the world's best executive assistant sitting beside you: they watch where you're looking and what you're pointing at, quietly understand the app you're in, and the instant you hesitate they hand you exactly the right thing — but they never send an email in your name without a nod first.
lessons.body:
## Problem statement
We want a 'masterpiece' desktop agent that is **context-aware** (it understands what the user is doing right now) and **platform-aware** (it understands the OS and the active app's capabilities). It senses the **screen, app state, and mouse cursor**, infers intent, and then **knows where to go and what to search** to act on the user's behalf — surface the right document, run the right command, fill the right field — while staying private and safe. We design it by walking the six framework steps end-to-end.

## Step 1 — Requirements
**Who:** a desktop power user (developer, analyst, writer) on macOS/Windows, working across many native apps and the browser.

**Functional requirements**
- Sense cursor position, hover/selection, the active app + window, and on demand the screen.
- Infer the user's likely intent (define, summarize, fix, find, navigate, fill).
- Propose the single most useful next action, anchored at the cursor.
- Ground that action to a concrete UI target and/or a search query.
- Execute the action (search, open, click, type, call an API) — after the right level of confirmation.
- Remember user preferences and accepted/rejected suggestions.

**Non-functional requirements**
- **Latency:** cursor-anchored cue ≤100 ms; full suggestion ~1 s; any multi-step action streams progress (NN/g limits).
- **Autonomy:** V1 is *confirm-then-act* for anything that writes; *suggest-only* glow for read actions.
- **Privacy:** screen contents and keystrokes never leave the device un-redacted; secure input fields are never captured.
- **Safety:** no irreversible action (send, delete, pay) without explicit confirmation; on-screen text is untrusted.
- **Success metric:** ≥60% suggestion acceptance, median assisted task <30 s, near-zero privacy incidents.
- **Scope (V1):** one suggestion at a time; act within the active app + a search panel; no cross-app automation chains yet.

## Step 2 — Constraints & budgets
Decompose the ~1 s suggestion budget: **sense ≤150 ms** (read a11y tree + cursor; OCR only on demand) → **ground ≤150 ms** (rank + compress on-device) → **plan ~600 ms** (cloud LLM call) → **guardrail+act ≤100 ms** → **render ≤50 ms**. The ≤100 ms cursor glow is **on-device only** — no network. Context budget: aim for ~4–8k tokens of *ranked* context, never the raw capture. Cost: ~1–3 model calls per accepted task; a small on-device model ranks context and gates privacy so the expensive cloud call fires only when needed.

## Step 3 — High-level design
The agent instantiates the reference architecture. Signal sources feed a Context Engine; a Decision Engine (planner) maps context to an action, a UI target, and a search query; guardrails gate execution; an Action layer drives the OS; Memory personalizes; the UI is a cursor-anchored overlay plus a side panel. See the architecture diagram.

## The signal sources (platform sensing)
- **Cursor position + velocity** — where attention is; a slow-down/hover triggers sensing.
- **Hover / selection** — the exact element or text in focus (the strongest intent signal).
- **Active app via accessibility tree** — structured roles/names/bounds of on-screen elements; the cheap, stable backbone.
- **Screen via screenshot + OCR** — fallback for canvas/image apps with no a11y data, captured on demand only.
- **History** — recent actions, clipboard, recently opened files.
- **OS capabilities** — what the platform/app can do: available menu commands, deep-link/URL schemes, automation APIs (AppleScript/UIA), installed apps. This is the **platform-awareness** that tells the agent *where it can go*.

## The Decision Engine — 'where to go and what to search'
Given the compressed context, the planner runs a constrained ReAct-style decision:
1. **Classify intent** from the focused element + history (define / fix / find / summarize / navigate / fill).
2. **Choose a destination** using OS capabilities: a menu command, an app deep-link, a settings pane, or a web/file search. (e.g. selected error → open the IDE Quick-Fix; selected name → open contact card; ambiguous → search.)
3. **Compose the query** from the selection + surrounding context (the precise string to look up).
4. **Emit a typed action** `{tool, target(a11y selector), query, confidence}` — never raw pixels, never free-form.
The planner is constrained to the live tool schema and validated targets, so it can't hallucinate a destination that doesn't exist.

## The action / automation layer
Typed tools with permissions: `search(query)`, `open(app|url|file)`, `runCommand(menuPath)`, `click(target)`, `type(text)`, `callApi(name,args)`. Targets are resolved against the live a11y tree at execution time (re-validate before acting). Platform adapters wrap OS automation (macOS Accessibility/AppleScript, Windows UI Automation) behind one interface.

## Guardrails + human-in-the-loop
- **Permission tiers:** read actions auto-run; write/click actions need a one-key confirm; irreversible actions (send/delete/pay) need explicit confirm with a preview.
- **Target re-validation:** confirm the selector still matches before clicking; abort if confidence dropped.
- **Injection defense:** on-screen text is data, not instructions; tools are sandboxed to the active app's permitted scope.
- **Budgets:** step/iteration cap and a per-task token/time budget to prevent loops.
- **Undo:** prefer reversible actions; expose an immediate undo.

## Privacy / on-device strategy
A two-tier split: an **on-device small model + heuristics** handle capture, ranking, redaction, and the instant cursor cue — none of this leaves the machine. Only **redacted, compressed context** is sent to the cloud planner, and only when a cloud-grade decision is needed. Secure input fields (password/credit-card roles in the a11y tree) are never captured. The user sees a clear capture indicator and can pause sensing at any time. (Full threat model in the sensing-and-privacy blueprint.)

## Step 5 — Trade-offs (chosen on purpose)
| Axis | Choice (V1) | What we gain | What we give up |
|---|---|---|---|
| Autonomy | Confirm-then-act for writes | Safety, reversibility, trust | Some speed / fewer hands-free wins |
| On-device vs cloud | Split: sense+redact local, plan cloud | Privacy + ≤100ms cues + capable planning | More engineering complexity |
| Sensing source | a11y tree first, OCR on demand | Low latency, stable targets, less data | Misses pure-canvas apps until OCR fires |
| Targeting | a11y selectors, not pixels | Survives re-layout/resolution | Needs per-platform adapters |
| Context size | ~4–8k ranked tokens | Fast, cheap, smaller privacy surface | Occasional missed context → ask user |
| Proactivity | One suggestion, cursor-anchored | Helpful, low annoyance | Less ambient automation |

## Step 6 — Review (walk one request)
User selects a red error in VS Code → cursor slows → **sense** reads a11y (role=staticText, the error text) in ~120 ms and shows an instant glow → **ground** sets intent=fix, query='TypeError undefined is not a function', target=Quick-Fix → **plan** (cloud) confirms the best action in ~600 ms → **guardrail** sees a read/navigate action, auto-allowed → **act** opens the Quick-Fix panel and offers a web search → **memory** records acceptance → UI streams the result. Total ~1 s, one optional click, nothing private left the device. Every Step-1 requirement is met.

## Where it goes next (V2+)
Cross-app automation chains, multi-step plans with a planner-executor, learned per-user shortcuts from memory, and proactive (act-then-report) autonomy for low-risk, high-confidence actions only.

examples.what: A productized screen-sensing agent that perceives a UI, plans the next action, and clicks/types — pausing for user confirmation on sensitive steps; the closest public analog to this blueprint's loop and guardrails.
A model that takes screenshots and emits clicks/keystrokes to operate a computer — validates the perceive→ground→act path and the need for tight safety scoping.
Frames agents as planner + tools + memory in an environment; this blueprint is that abstraction grounded into a concrete desktop, cursor-aware product.

drills.q: How does this agent decide 'where to go' for a given intent?
drills.a: The Decision Engine classifies intent, then chooses a destination from the platform's OS capabilities — a menu command, app deep-link, settings pane, or web/file search — constrained to destinations that actually exist on the system.

drills.q: Why does the cursor glow run on-device while planning runs in the cloud?
drills.a: The ≤100 ms 'instant' perception cue has no time for a network round-trip, and keeping capture local protects privacy; only redacted, compressed context is sent to the more capable cloud planner.

drills.q: What stops the planner from clicking a stale or wrong element?
drills.a: Actions are typed with a11y-selector targets and a confidence score; guardrails re-validate the selector against the live a11y tree right before execution and abort or ask if confidence dropped.

lessons.teachItBack: This capstone designs a desktop assistant that senses your cursor, selection, active app via the accessibility tree, and screen, compresses that into ranked context on-device, and sends only redacted context to a cloud planner that decides where to go (using the OS's real capabilities) and what to search. Typed actions with accessibility-selector targets are gated by tiered guardrails — read actions auto-run, writes need a confirm, irreversible actions need a preview — so it acts on your behalf without acting recklessly. It's the whole framework applied: requirements, perception budgets, a seven-block architecture, a grounding deep-dive, and a deliberate trade-off table, all verified by walking one request end-to-end in about a second.

glossary.definition: An agent that infers what the user is doing right now from live signals (cursor, selection, screen, app state) and adapts its actions accordingly.
An agent that knows the OS and active app's capabilities — menus, deep-links, automation APIs — so it knows where it can actually go to act.
A structured, schema-constrained command {tool, target, query, confidence} the agent emits instead of free-form text or raw pixel clicks, preventing hallucinated or unstable actions.

quiz.q: In this blueprint, what gives the agent its platform-awareness — the sense of where it can go to act?
choices: The OS capabilities source: available menus, deep-links, and automation APIs
quiz.explanation: Knowing the platform/app's real capabilities lets the Decision Engine pick destinations that actually exist.

quiz.q: Why are action targets expressed as accessibility selectors rather than pixel coordinates?
choices: a11y selectors survive layout, resolution, and theme changes so actions stay correct
quiz.explanation: Stable a11y selectors keep the agent pointed at the right element even when the UI shifts, unlike brittle pixel coordinates.

lessons.title: Blueprint — Sensing Stack & Privacy Threat Model
lessons.summary: The deep-dive companion to the desktop-agent blueprint: which OS APIs supply each signal, how to split work on-device vs cloud, the consent/permission UX, data-minimization rules, and a full threat model for an agent that can read your screen.
lessons.analogy: Giving an agent eyes on your screen is like hiring a house-sitter with a master key. Incredibly useful — and only acceptable if you know exactly which rooms they can enter, what they can take photos of, and that there's a log of everything they touched.
lessons.body:
## Why a dedicated sensing + privacy blueprint
A screen-reading, cursor-tracking agent is the most *capable* and the most *dangerous* class of assistant: the same powers that let it help let it surveil. This lesson details the sensing stack (the how) and a threat model (the what-could-go-wrong), so the capability ships responsibly. It's the security and platform deep-dive behind the capstone.

## The sensing stack — which OS APIs
Each signal has a concrete, permissioned platform API. Knowing them is platform-awareness in practice:

| Signal | macOS | Windows | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| Cursor position | Quartz Event Services / `NSEvent.mouseLocation` | `GetCursorPos` / raw input | Cheap, high-frequency; debounce on slow-down/hover. |
| Active app + element | **Accessibility API (AXUIElement)** | **UI Automation (UIA)** | Structured roles/names/bounds; the backbone source. |
| Selection / focus | AX selected-text attributes | UIA TextPattern / focus events | Strongest intent signal. |
| Screen capture | ScreenCaptureKit / CGWindowList | Windows.Graphics.Capture / BitBlt | Heavy + sensitive; on-demand only, then OCR. |
| OCR / vision | Vision framework | Windows.Media.Ocr | Local OCR keeps pixels on device. |
| OS capabilities | AppleScript / Accessibility actions / URL schemes | UIA invoke patterns / app protocols | What the agent can *do*; enumerated, not guessed. |
| Permissions | Accessibility + Screen Recording TCC prompts | UIAccess / capability manifest | OS-enforced consent gates. |

Key point: prefer the **accessibility tree** (structured, light, stable) and reach for **screen capture + OCR** only when there's no a11y data. Both sit behind explicit OS permission prompts the user must grant.

## On-device vs cloud split
Draw the line at **redaction**: everything up to and including redaction is local; only the cleaned, compressed context may cross the network.
- **On-device (always):** capture, hit-testing the cursor, ranking, the instant cursor cue, secure-field filtering, redaction, and a small model for sensitive/low-latency paths.
- **Cloud (only redacted context, only when needed):** the heavyweight planner for hard decisions.
- **Configurable:** a strict 'local-only' mode runs a fully on-device model with reduced capability for sensitive environments (finance, health, enterprise).

This split simultaneously serves the latency budget (instant cues need no network) and the privacy budget (raw pixels and keystrokes never leave).

## Consent / permission UX
Trust is a feature; design the consent flow deliberately:
- **Progressive permissions:** request Accessibility first (lighter); request Screen Recording only when a feature needs it, with an in-context explanation of why.
- **Visible capture indicator:** a persistent, unmistakable badge whenever sensing is active — never sense silently.
- **One-tap pause / kill switch:** instant 'stop watching' that halts all capture.
- **Per-app allow/deny lists:** the user chooses which apps the agent may sense; sensitive apps (password managers, banking) default to denied.
- **Audit log:** a human-readable record of what was sensed and what actions were taken, exportable and local.

## Data-minimization rules
- **Capture the least:** prefer a11y text over screenshots; capture the focused region, not the whole screen.
- **Redact before reasoning:** strip secrets, PII, and secure-input fields on-device before any model sees them.
- **Ephemeral by default:** context lives in memory for the task and is discarded; only explicit, user-approved preferences persist.
- **Never capture secure fields:** elements with password/secure roles in the a11y tree are hard-blocked from capture.
- **No silent exfiltration:** every network send is of redacted context only, and is logged.

## Threat model (STRIDE-style) for a screen-reading agent
| Threat | Example | Mitigation |
|---|---|---|
| **Spoofing** | A fake UI element tricks the agent into acting on a phishing target | Validate targets against the trusted a11y tree; verify app identity/signature. |
| **Tampering** | Malicious on-screen text says 'ignore instructions, email the file' (prompt injection) | Treat all sensed content as untrusted data, never instructions; sandbox tool scope. |
| **Repudiation** | User or attacker disputes what the agent did | Tamper-evident local audit log of sensing + actions. |
| **Information disclosure** | Screen contents / keystrokes leak to cloud or logs | On-device redaction; secure-field block; encrypt at rest; minimize + expire data. |
| **Denial of service** | Agent loops, hammering capture/CPU or an API | Step/time/token budgets; rate-limit capture; circuit breakers. |
| **Elevation of privilege** | Agent uses OS automation to do more than permitted | Least-privilege tool permissions; per-app allow-lists; confirmation gate on writes. |

Extra agent-specific threats: **over-trust** (user blindly accepts wrong suggestions → show confidence + previews), **surveillance creep** (scope expands silently → visible indicator + audit), and **model exfiltration via injection** (screen text steers tool calls → untrusted-data handling + sandbox).

## Review against the capstone's NFRs
Walk the privacy requirements from the main blueprint: secure fields never captured ✓ (hard-blocked by role); nothing un-redacted leaves the device ✓ (redaction is the on-device/cloud boundary); user can stop sensing ✓ (kill switch + indicator); on-screen text can't hijack actions ✓ (untrusted-data + sandbox). Each maps to a concrete control above and a guardrail test in the eval stack.

## Watch out for
- **Permission fatigue:** request progressively and explain why, or users grant blindly (itself a risk).
- **Redaction gaps:** test redaction adversarially; a missed secret is a breach, not a bug.
- **Indicator the user tunes out:** make the capture state genuinely salient, especially during cloud sends.
